proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that applies a single explanation method holistically, our approach explicitly decomposes the explanation task according to the model's inherent computational graph, enabling the integration of diverse local methods into a structurally coherent global explanation.

proposals.title: Meta-Explanations: Few-Shot Adaptation of Sparse Linear Explanations for Novel Subpopulations in Sequential and Generative Models
proposals.motivation: As machine learning models are deployed in dynamic, real-world settings, they encounter novel, underrepresented data subpopulations (e.g., new linguistic styles or visual concepts). Pre-2026, post-hoc explanation methods like LIME or SHAP are static and require significant data to produce faithful explanations for new groups, failing to provide timely interpretability for model failures or emergent behaviors. This gap hinders robust debugging and erodes trust, especially for complex sequential and generative models where failure modes are poorly understood.
proposals.hypothesis: The process of generating a faithful, sparse linear explanation for a model's prediction on a novel data subgroup can be framed as a few-shot learning task, and a meta-learned explanation mechanism can rapidly adapt to new subpopulations using only a handful of examples without model retraining.
proposals.method: We will develop a model-agnostic meta-learning framework where the inner-loop task is to learn parameters for a sparse linear explainer (e.g., a variant of LIME) on a support set from a specific data subgroup. The outer loop will meta-train the explanation algorithm's initialization and adaptation rule across many diverse subgroups, sampled from a broad base training distribution. For sequential models (LSTMs), we will meta-learn over token- or span-level attributions; for generative models (GANs), we will focus on latent space interventions. The framework will be evaluated on benchmarks containing emerging subpopulations, using fidelity and stability metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical tool for continuous model auditing in non-stationary environments, enabling rapid diagnosis of performance on novel groups. It will contribute a new perspective on explanation as an adaptive, few-shot process and offer insights into how model behavior generalizes across emerging data distributions.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been applied for model adaptation, its use for rapidly adapting *post-hoc explanation mechanisms* themselves is novel. This approach synergizes Santurkar's prior work on synthetic data generation (ARISE) and analyzing emergent model properties by creating an explanatory framework specifically for unforeseen data shifts.

proposals.title: Sparse Linear Explanations for Sequential and Generative Models: Extending Interpretability Frameworks to RNNs and GANs
proposals.motivation: Sparse linear explanation methods, such as those used for feature attribution in static models, have proven valuable for interpreting knowledge and decision rules in pre-trained language models. However, their application is largely limited to feed-forward architectures, leaving a gap in understanding complex, sequential dynamics in RNNs and the latent space manipulations in generative models like GANs. Extending these interpretability tools is crucial for auditing, debugging, and distilling knowledge from these widely used but opaque model families, especially as synthetic data generation becomes more prevalent.
proposals.hypothesis: The core principles of sparse linear explanation can be effectively generalized to model the temporal dependencies in RNNs and the feature disentanglement in GANs, producing human-interpretable rules that capture their sequential and generative behaviors.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the sparse linear explanation framework (e.g., LIME or post-hoc rule induction) for sequential data. For RNNs (e.g., LSTMs, GRUs), we will treat hidden state trajectories as features for explanation, using perturbation-based methods on input sequences to induce sparse rules over time steps. For GANs, we will apply the method to the generator's latent space, perturbing latent vectors and observing output changes in the synthetic data to derive linear rules linking latent dimensions to specific visual or semantic attributes. The approach will be validated on established benchmarks for text generation (using RNNs) and image synthesis (using GANs like StyleGAN2).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified methodological extension for interpreting two critical model classes, enabling new analyses of emergent temporal and generative properties. It will yield practical tools for dataset curation and model distillation by identifying compact, human-readable rules governing model behavior, advancing the goal of building more transparent and controllable AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While sparse explanations are common for classifiers, applying them to model the internal sequential dynamics of RNNs and the causal structure of GAN latent spaces is a novel direction. This moves beyond analyzing static outputs to interpreting the generative and temporal processes themselves.

proposals.title: Negative Selection in Neural Networks: An Immunological Approach to Debiasing via Explanation-Guided Regularization
proposals.motivation: Machine learning models, especially large language models, often learn to exploit spurious correlations and societal biases present in their training data, leading to unfair and brittle performance. Existing debiasing methods, such as data augmentation or adversarial training, can be computationally expensive or struggle to generalize. A more fundamental, optimization-level approach is needed to prevent models from forming 'detectors' for these harmful features in the first place.
proposals.hypothesis: By treating known spurious feature patterns as 'self' in an immunological sense and applying a continuous negative selection pressure during training, we can prevent a model from allocating representational capacity to them, forcing it to learn more robust and invariant features instead.
proposals.method: We will integrate a negative selection module into the standard training loop. For each batch, we will generate sparse local explanation maps (using efficient variants of SHAP or LIME) to identify input features the model is using for its prediction. Features flagged as spurious (e.g., demographic indicators, syntactic shortcuts) by a predefined rule set or a bias lexicon are treated as 'self'. We will then compute the correlation between these 'self' explanation maps and the activation patterns of internal model components (e.g., attention heads, intermediate layer neurons). A regularization penalty proportional to this correlation is added to the loss, effectively censoring components that react to 'self'.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, biologically-inspired optimization framework for intrinsic model debiasing that directly uses explanation feedback. It will provide a new method for creating models that are more robust to spurious correlations without relying solely on curated external data. We will validate the approach on established NLP benchmarks for fairness and out-of-distribution generalization.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the direct analogy to the negative selection algorithm from artificial immune systems, translating it into an explanation-guided regularization penalty applied during gradient-based optimization, which is distinct from post-hoc correction or adversarial data filtering.

proposals.title: Concept-Bottlenecked Diffusion: Interpretable and Controllable Synthesis via Human-Defined Concept Intervention
proposals.motivation: Modern multimodal generative models (e.g., diffusion models) are powerful but operate as black boxes, making it difficult to align their outputs with precise human intent or debug systematic failures. This lack of interpretability and fine-grained control limits their safe and reliable application in domains requiring high precision. A method that grounds generation in a sparse set of human-understandable concepts would enable direct steering and correction of the generative process.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a trainable concept bottleneck layer—where activations correspond to human-defined semantic attributes—into a multimodal generative architecture, we can create a system where intervention on these concept activations allows for precise, interpretable control and debugging of the synthesized outputs, improving both alignment and transparency.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained text-to-image diffusion model (e.g., Stable Diffusion) by inserting a concept bottleneck module between its text encoder and the denoising U-Net. This module will be trained using a combination of (1) a small dataset of concept-annotated images and (2) a larger corpus of synthetic concept labels generated via rule-based induction from captions (inspired by ARISE). Training will enforce that the bottleneck layer's sparse activations predict the target concepts and are causally influential for generation via a concept-alignment loss. At inference, users can inspect, clamp, or edit these concept values to steer synthesis.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel generative model architecture that offers unprecedented interpretability and intervention capabilities for multimodal synthesis. It will provide a framework for debugging and correcting model failures at the conceptual level, advancing the alignment and safety of generative AI. The methodology will also contribute new techniques for integrating symbolic, rule-derived knowledge with large-scale neural generative processes.
proposals.novelty: While concept bottlenecks have been explored for classification, their application as a direct control and debugging interface for *generative* models is novel. This approach uniquely combines rule-based synthetic data generation for concept labeling with intervention mechanisms in a diffusion framework to achieve human-in-the-loop steerable synthesis.

proposals.title: Faithful Explanations via Neurosymbolic Alignment: Synthesizing Programs that Match Model Predictions and Internal States
proposals.motivation: Explaining complex, black-box models like large language models (LLMs) remains a critical challenge. Current post-hoc explanation methods, such as feature attribution or concept-based analyses, often lack faithfulness, meaning they fail to reflect the model's true internal reasoning process. A faithful, model-agnostic explanation framework is needed to build trust and enable debugging, especially as models are deployed in high-stakes domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synthesizing symbolic programs whose execution traces align with a target model's activation patterns, in addition to matching its input-output behavior, will yield more faithful and interpretable explanations than methods relying solely on input-output agreement.
proposals.method: We will develop a neurosymbolic program synthesis system. It uses a domain-specific language (DSL) to generate candidate explanation programs. A search algorithm (e.g., evolutionary or reinforcement learning-based) will optimize programs on a dual-objective loss: (1) output fidelity, measuring match between program and model predictions, and (2) state alignment, using a learned mapping (e.g., a linear probe or small neural network) between the program's internal variable states and the model's intermediate layer activations. The system treats the model as a black-box oracle for querying predictions and activations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for generating model-agnostic, rule-based explanations with a stronger guarantee of faithfulness. It will provide a new benchmark for evaluating explanation fidelity beyond predictive accuracy. The method also advances neurosymbolic AI by framing explanation as an alignment search problem between symbolic and neural computational states.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior rule distillation work that assumes sparse weight encodings, our approach enforces faithfulness via direct alignment of internal computational states. It is also distinct from purely behavioral program synthesis, as it uses the model's activation dynamics as a supervisory signal for explanation search.

proposals.title: Stratify: Extracting Hierarchical Symbolic Rules from Sparse Neural Networks via Geological Analogy
proposals.motivation: The opacity of large neural networks, especially in high-stakes domains, necessitates methods for extracting human-interpretable rules. However, existing rule extraction and program synthesis techniques often struggle with fidelity or scalability when dealing with the sparse, distributed representations in modern models. This work addresses the core challenge of reconstructing a complete, faithful decision logic from the sparse 'samples' of a network's internal activations.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting geological principles of stratigraphy—specifically superposition (layered formation) and lateral continuity (rule consistency across the input space)—to the structure of sparse neural network weights and activations, we can algorithmically reconstruct hierarchical, symbolic rule sets that accurately approximate the model's decision boundaries.
proposals.method: We will treat sparse activation patterns from a frozen model (e.g., a transformer) on a diverse dataset as 'core samples.' Inspired by ARISE, we will develop an iterative rule induction algorithm. This algorithm will use a continuity constraint, analogous to geological lateral continuity, to hypothesize and validate rule completions across semantically similar inputs. We will synthesize data to test rule hypotheses, employing length-diversity-aware sampling for robustness. The final output will be a layered rule hierarchy, where lower-level rules (early layers) combine via logical operations into higher-level decision rules.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, principled framework for generating high-fidelity, hierarchical symbolic explanations from sparse neural networks. It will advance the field of interpretable AI by offering a method that bridges the performance of neural models with the transparency of rule-based systems. The resulting tool will enable better model auditing and knowledge distillation.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct conceptual and algorithmic translation of geological stratigraphy principles to the problem of neural network interpretability. While rule induction and program synthesis exist, this geological analogy provides a new formal framework for reasoning about sparse, distributed representations as incomplete samples of a coherent underlying 'decision geology.'

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Robust and Explainable Models: Compute-Optimal Distillation into Symbolic Rules
proposals.motivation: Scaling laws have demonstrated predictable performance gains with increased compute and data for large neural networks, but these models remain black boxes vulnerable to distribution shifts and adversarial examples. There is a critical need for models that are both robust and interpretable for high-stakes applications, yet current methods treat robustness and explainability as separate challenges. This work addresses the core trade-off by investigating how to systematically produce robust, transparent models from large-scale compute.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there exists a compute-optimal frontier for training robust feature extractors, and that the robust features learned at this frontier can be effectively distilled via program synthesis into a compact set of human-readable symbolic rules without sacrificing adversarial robustness or out-of-distribution performance.
proposals.method: We will first establish scaling laws for training robust feature extractors (e.g., vision transformers or robustly fine-tuned LLMs) on a fixed massive dataset like LAION or C4, measuring robustness to distribution shifts and adversarial attacks. Building on methods from ARISE, we will then use iterative rule induction and synthetic data generation, guided by the robust model's features and gradients, to synthesize a decision program. This program will be refined using techniques from neurosymbolic program synthesis to ensure fidelity to the robust model's behavior while maintaining conciseness and readability.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) novel scaling laws for robust model training, 2) a systematic framework for distilling large, robust models into explainable rule-based systems, and 3) a new benchmark for evaluating the robustness-explainability trade-off across compute scales. It will provide a principled path to building high-performance, auditable models.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws and rule distillation have been studied separately, their intersection for robust and explainable models is unexplored. The novelty lies in framing compute-optimal scaling as a precursor to explainable model synthesis, creating a direct pipeline from massive compute to transparent, reliable decision logic.

proposals.title: INCODE: Influence-Guided Neural Architecture Search for Interpretable and Robust Neural Codecs
proposals.motivation: Neural codecs for audio and image compression are typically designed for efficiency and rate-distortion performance, but their black-box nature obscures failure modes and sensitivity to training data artifacts. This lack of transparency is a critical barrier to deployment in high-stakes or safety-critical domains. Furthermore, standard Neural Architecture Search (NAS) optimizes only for performance metrics, potentially selecting architectures that are brittle or overly reliant on spurious correlations in the training set.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating influence function-based diagnostics into the NAS loop for encoder-decoder pairs will steer the search towards architectures that are not only performant but also inherently more robust and interpretable, as measured by reduced sensitivity to outlier training samples and more traceable prediction pathways.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel NAS framework where candidate architectures (e.g., variations of autoencoder blocks, attention mechanisms, and quantization modules) are evaluated on a multi-objective loss combining rate-distortion with an influence robustness penalty. For each candidate, we will compute influence functions (using efficient approximations like TracIn) on a held-out diagnostic set to quantify its sensitivity to small, noisy, or synthetic outlier samples. This influence profile will be used to penalize architectures with high, concentrated influence from few data points. The search will be conducted using a differentiable NAS (DARTS) or evolutionary strategy over a tailored search space for neural codecs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new methodology for co-designing model architecture and explainability, yielding neural codecs with documented robustness characteristics. It will also contribute a benchmark for evaluating the influence stability of compression models and release a set of architecturally transparent codecs for audio/image domains.
proposals.novelty: While influence functions have been used for post-hoc analysis and data curation, their integration as a real-time, guiding objective within NAS for codec design is novel. This moves beyond using synthetic data for augmentation (ARISE) to using influence diagnostics on data—including synthetic outliers—to directly shape architecture discovery.

proposals.title: PASPLE: Physics-Constrained Sparse Explanations for Robust Vision Models
proposals.motivation: Current pixel-level explanation methods like LIME or SHAP often identify sparse but physically implausible feature sets, limiting their utility for diagnosing model failures or ensuring robustness. Simultaneously, data augmentation for robustness testing is often generic (e.g., random noise) and not grounded in realistic feature variations. There is a critical need for explanation frameworks that are both interpretable in the raw input space and inherently tied to plausible real-world transformations.
proposals.hypothesis: By constraining sparse pixel-level explainers with transformations from physics-based simulators, we can generate model explanations that are more robust, semantically meaningful, and better predictors of a model's failure modes under realistic distribution shifts.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we will use differentiable physics simulators (e.g., for lighting, texture, or simple rigid-body motion) to generate a distribution of plausible augmented samples around a given input. Second, we will adapt a sparse linear explainer (e.g., based on LASSO or orthogonal matching pursuit) to operate within this constrained feature space, identifying a minimal set of pixels whose values, when perturbed along these physical dimensions, most significantly impact the model's prediction. The method will be evaluated on vision benchmarks using both explanation fidelity metrics and downstream robustness tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel paradigm for generating physically-grounded explanations, moving beyond adversarial or noise-based probes. It will provide a new diagnostic tool for model robustness by linking interpretability directly to plausible failure modes. The framework will also advance methods for generating targeted, semantically meaningful synthetic data for model testing.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the fusion of unconstrained, high-dimensional sparse explanation techniques with a structured, physics-constrained search space for perturbations, creating a bridge between raw pixel interpretability and semantically meaningful feature variation. This differs from prior work that either applies explainers post-hoc or uses physics simulators solely for augmentation without explanation.

proposals.title: Inverse Feature Synthesis: Generating Inputs to Activate Sparse Explanatory Concepts in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Modern interpretability methods, such as sparse autoencoders or concept bottleneck models, identify sparse, human-interpretable features within neural networks. However, understanding what inputs *cause* these features to activate remains a challenging inverse problem. This gap limits our ability to validate, debug, and causally understand model representations, a core challenge in pre-2026 interpretability research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an iterative synthesis-and-test framework, inspired by inverse spectroscopy, can efficiently generate prototypical and counterfactual text inputs that reliably and specifically activate a target sparse explanatory feature, thereby elucidating its precise semantic role.
proposals.method: We will adapt the ARISE framework's iterative rule induction and synthetic data generation loop. For a target feature (e.g., a sparse autoencoder latent), we will use a fine-tuned language model as a generator to produce candidate text. We will measure feature activation in the model under analysis, then optimize the generator via policy gradient methods (e.g., REINFORCE) to minimize the difference between the observed and target activation signature. This loop will be refined using rules distilled from successful candidates to improve specificity and diversity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel tool for controlled exploration of model internals, enabling researchers to generate targeted datasets for feature validation. It will contribute a methodology for scaling the inverse mapping from explanations to inputs, advancing beyond static feature analysis towards interactive interpretability.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes or distills existing model features, our approach actively *synthesizes* inputs to target them, framing interpretability as an inverse design problem. This synthesis-driven validation of sparse features is distinct from passive observational studies.

proposals.title: RISE: Robust and Sparse Explanations for Edge AI via Adversarially-Stable Feature Attribution
proposals.motivation: As neural networks are increasingly deployed on resource-constrained edge devices, there is a critical need for trustworthy, real-time model introspection. Existing explanation methods like gradient-based saliency maps are computationally lightweight but notoriously fragile to small, often adversarial, input perturbations, which undermines their reliability for security-critical debugging and monitoring. This gap is acute in real-world edge settings where both computational resources are limited and input noise or manipulation is common.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by fusing principles from robust statistics (e.g., using a loss based on influence functions or M-estimators) with sparse approximation techniques (e.g., L1-constrained attribution), we can generate feature attributions that are simultaneously sparse, computationally efficient, and stable under input perturbations, thereby providing faithful explanations for edge-deployed models.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel adversarially-robust loss function that penalizes the divergence between explanations for a clean input and its perturbed versions, drawing on pre-2026 work in robust optimization. This loss will guide an iterative, rule-induction-inspired procedure to distill a sparse set of critical features, ensuring the explanation is both interpretable and stable. The framework will be implemented as a lightweight post-hoc wrapper, compatible with common edge architectures, and evaluated on benchmarks for model debugging under adversarial attacks and natural noise.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a unified, practical framework for robust explainability on edge devices, directly addressing the dual constraints of resource efficiency and adversarial resilience. It will provide new evaluation benchmarks for explanation robustness and offer a method for real-time model debugging that enhances trust in edge AI systems deployed in noisy or security-sensitive environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of robust statistical guarantees with sparsity constraints for explanation generation, a combination not explored in pre-2026 XAI literature, which typically addresses computational efficiency and robustness as separate challenges.

proposals.title: Directed Evolution of Model Behavior: Black-Box Editing via Adversarial Counterfactual Probes
proposals.motivation: As proprietary and large-scale models are increasingly accessed only via API, the ability to understand and correct their behaviors without internal access is crucial. Current black-box probing methods are largely diagnostic, and effective, interpretable editing remains an open challenge, limiting safe and reliable deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model's input-output behavior can be systematically and locally edited by iteratively evolving a population of minimal adversarial perturbations under selective pressure for a target output, resulting in an external, transferable 'counterfactual probe'.
proposals.method: We will adapt the ARISE framework's iterative rule induction and synthetic data generation cycle. Starting with a seed input, we will generate a diverse population of counterfactual examples using constrained text perturbations (e.g., synonym swaps, syntactic changes). We query the black-box model, selecting only perturbations that induce the desired output change (e.g., flipping a class label). This selected set informs the induction of a formal 'editing rule' (e.g., a pattern or transformation), which guides the next cycle of synthetic data generation, iteratively refining the probe. The final output is a curated dataset or lightweight transformation function that applies the evolved edit.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, API-only method for targeted model editing, contributing a new paradigm inspired by evolutionary biology. It will yield interpretable counterfactual probes that offer insights into model decision boundaries and enable controlled behavioral adjustments without retraining.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in framing black-box editing as a directed evolution process, distinct from gradient-based white-box editing or static dataset curation. It uniquely combines iterative synthetic data generation with selective pressure to evolve edits, rather than merely discovering them.

proposals.title: NAS for Editability: Co-Designing Architectures with Provable Guarantees for Rule-Based Model Editing
proposals.motivation: Post-hoc rule editing of neural networks (e.g., via direct parameter manipulation) is crucial for updating model knowledge without full retraining, but it often leads to unintended side-effects and catastrophic forgetting. Current methods apply edits to arbitrary, pre-existing architectures not designed for modification, lacking formal guarantees on edit stability. This creates a reliability bottleneck for deploying updatable models in dynamic, knowledge-intensive domains like language modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating formal metrics of edit stability directly into a Neural Architecture Search (NAS) objective, we can discover model architectures that are inherently more amenable to safe, localized rule updates while maintaining high task performance.
proposals.method: We will define a NAS search space over encoder-decoder and classifier components, including layer types, activation functions, and connectivity patterns. The reward function will combine standard performance metrics (e.g., accuracy on a held-out set) with novel, differentiable proxies for theoretical editability, such as bounds on output distortion in relevant subspaces after simulated parameter edits. We will employ a differentiable NAS (DARTS) framework, augmented with a regularization term derived from a stability analysis of the model's Jacobian with respect to rule-encoding parameters, to efficiently search for optimal architectures.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a novel NAS framework that optimizes for architectural editability, 2) a set of discovered architectures with provable stability guarantees for a class of rule edits, and 3) a public benchmark for evaluating the trade-off between model performance and post-hoc edit safety, advancing the field beyond ad-hoc editing methods.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used NAS for performance or robustness, and separate work has developed editing algorithms, this proposal is the first to co-design architecture and editing theory, using NAS to proactively construct models for reliable modification rather than attempting to edit arbitrary networks post-hoc.

proposals.title: Projective Alignment: Inference-Time Constraint Enforcement via Differentiable Projection on Frozen Language Models
proposals.motivation: Large pre-trained language models (LLMs) often violate critical fairness, safety, or robustness constraints, and post-hoc correction methods like fine-tuning can degrade performance or fail to guarantee constraint satisfaction. Existing rule-based or prompting interventions lack trainable, minimal interventions that preserve the model's core knowledge. A method is needed to enforce constraints with guarantees while maintaining the original model's accuracy and capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight, trainable projection module, applied to a frozen LLM's internal representations or logits during inference, can be optimized to strictly enforce specified constraints while minimizing deviation from the model's original predictions, thereby achieving guaranteed alignment without catastrophic forgetting.
proposals.method: We propose a small neural projection head that takes intermediate layer activations or final logits from a frozen base model (e.g., GPT-2, T5). This module is trained with a bi-objective loss: a strict penalty (e.g., Lagrangian, constrained optimization) for constraint violation (e.g., demographic parity, adversarial robustness) and a strong regularization term (e.g., KL divergence) penalizing changes to the original output distribution. Training uses synthetic or curated datasets (inspired by ARISE) to exemplify constraints, and the module is optimized via gradient-based methods to perform a minimal 'correction' at inference time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, parameter-efficient framework for guaranteed constraint enforcement in LLMs without retraining, preserving pre-trained knowledge. It will contribute a new methodology for 'alignment as projection,' bridging rule-based systems and differentiable training, with benchmarks on fairness, robustness, and syntactic constraints.
proposals.novelty: Unlike fine-tuning or auxiliary networks, the method enforces constraints via a *differentiable inference-time projection*, a concept distinct from adversarial training or prompt engineering. It strictly decouples constraint satisfaction from model knowledge, ensuring the base model remains entirely frozen and unaltered.

proposals.title: Sparse, Constrained Model Editing via Iterative Rule Induction and Synthetic Data
proposals.motivation: Current methods for editing large language models (LLMs), such as fine-tuning or parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT), often modify a large number of parameters, risking catastrophic forgetting and loss of general capabilities. There is a critical need for surgical, interpretable interventions that can induce a specific behavioral shift (e.g., correcting a factual error or removing a bias) while preserving the model's original performance profile across a broad distribution of tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: A minimal, sparse perturbation to a model's parameters can be found by first inducing a rule-based representation of the target behavioral shift and then using synthetic data to guide a constrained optimization that isolates and edits only the most critical network components.
proposals.method: We will adapt the ARISE framework for iterative rule induction to define the target phenotype (e.g., 'do not generate harmful content when prompted with X'). These rules will guide the generation of targeted synthetic data pairs (input, desired output). We will then formulate a constrained optimization problem: minimize the L0 norm of the parameter delta subject to maximizing performance on the synthetic data and a robustness constraint enforcing minimal performance degradation on a diverse benchmark (e.g., MMLU, HELM). Optimization will leverage pre-2026 techniques like magnitude pruning, straight-through estimators for sparsity, and influence functions to identify candidate parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable algorithm for precise model editing, providing a formal framework for the minimal perturbation problem in neural networks. It will yield new insights into the localization of specific 'knowledge' or behaviors within model parameters and offer a more controlled alternative to full-model fine-tuning for safety and alignment tasks.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines rule-based synthetic data generation with a sparsity-constrained, robustness-preserving optimization, moving beyond gradient-based editing or rank-one updates by explicitly minimizing the edit size while enforcing broad functional retention via benchmarks.

proposals.title: Sparse Feature Editing for Causal Model Updates: A Dictionary Learning and Consistency Fine-Tuning Approach
proposals.motivation: Current methods for editing knowledge in large language models (LLMs), such as direct parameter updates or rank-one model editing, often lack precision, affecting unrelated capabilities or failing to generalize. A more fundamental, causal approach is needed to isolate and modify the specific sparse features underlying a concept, enabling reliable and localized edits. This is crucial for correcting model errors, updating factual knowledge, and improving safety without costly retraining.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying sparse dictionary learning to model activations will yield monosemantic feature bases, and that causal tracing can identify a sparse subset of these features responsible for a target behavior. Editing the read-out weights for these features, followed by consistency fine-tuning, will yield precise, effective, and minimally disruptive model edits.
proposals.method: We will apply a sparse autoencoder (SAE) to intermediate activations of a pre-trained LLM (e.g., LLaMA-2) to learn an overcomplete dictionary of interpretable features. For a target behavior (e.g., a fact or bias), we will use causal tracing/intervention to identify a small set of causally relevant dictionary features. The edit will be implemented by directly modifying the downstream weight matrix that reads out these features. Finally, we will perform lightweight fine-tuning using a consistency objective (e.g., distillation on a diverse benchmark suite) to preserve overall model performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, causality-grounded pipeline for precise model editing that operates on learned sparse features rather than neurons. It will provide new insights into the compositional structure of knowledge in LLMs and deliver a practical tool for making reliable, localized updates. The method will be rigorously evaluated on established benchmarks for editing efficacy, generalization, and specificity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of causal tracing with learned sparse dictionaries for editing, moving beyond neuron-level edits to a more interpretable and compositional feature space. The post-edit consistency fine-tuning phase is a key innovation to maintain overall model utility, directly building on the researcher's expertise in synthetic data and performance preservation.

ideas.title: Micro-HELM for Rapid, Cross-Algorithmic RL Prototyping and Analysis
ideas.description: This project develops Micro-HELM, a minimal, fast version of the HELM framework specifically designed for the rapid prototyping and testing of deep reinforcement learning algorithms. Its core innovation is the integration of a standardized, plug-and-play test suite that allows developers to instantly apply the same case study methodology and evaluation scenarios across multiple algorithms like DQN, SAC, and others. This enables direct, apples-to-apples comparison of emergent behaviors, sample efficiency, and robustness flaws during the early design phase. The synergy transforms isolated algorithm extension studies and model prototyping into a unified, iterative workflow for accelerated RL research and development.

ideas.title: Controlled Algorithmic Mechanism Disentanglement for Deep RL
ideas.description: This research develops a rigorous framework to isolate and compare the core mechanisms of deep RL algorithms like DQN, SAC, and others. It employs a standardized testing protocol with a shared codebase, matched hyperparameter tuning budgets, and a carefully constructed benchmark environment suite designed to stress specific algorithmic properties (e.g., value estimation, exploration, credit assignment). The analysis focuses on deriving causal relationships between algorithmic design choices and performance across different environmental dimensions, moving beyond simple performance tables to a mechanistic understanding of strengths and failure modes.

ideas.title: Invert goal: accuracy-first robust training
ideas.description: Start from high-accuracy models and add robustness with minimal standard performance loss.
ideas.sourceTitle: Robustness May Be at Odds with Accuracy

ideas.title: Compute-Optimal Synthesis of Robust, Explainable Rule-Based Models
ideas.description: This research direction seeks to find the compute-optimal scaling laws for training models to be robust to distribution shifts and adversarial examples on a fixed massive dataset, but with the explicit objective that the final model must be explainable. Instead of just scaling up black-box neural networks, the core is to use program synthesis techniques to distill the robust features learned at optimal compute scales into a set of concise, human-readable symbolic rules or decision programs. This synergy enables the systematic construction of robust models whose decision logic is transparent and auditable, addressing the core trade-off between scale, performance, and interpretability in modern machine learning.

ideas.title: Robust Data Selection for Sequential Decision-Making in Low-Resource Environments
ideas.description: This research direction develops a sample complexity analysis for robust reinforcement learning that is specifically tailored for low-resource settings. It adapts and downscales data selection algorithms like DSIR to sequentially and efficiently curate training data for robust RL agents, focusing on trajectories that are most informative for worst-case performance. The core synergy is a theoretical and algorithmic framework where data selection actively mitigates the distribution shift central to robust RL, but does so with a provably low computational budget for estimating the data selection features. This enables the practical deployment of robust sequential decision-makers in scenarios with both limited raw interaction data and constrained compute for offline data processing.

ideas.title: Refine scaling laws for more accurate performance prediction
ideas.description: Improve infrastructure and methods to predict model capabilities more precisely before full-scale training.
ideas.sourceTitle: GPT-4 Technical Report

ideas.title: Robust Sparse Explanations for Adversarially-Resilient Edge Debugging
ideas.description: This research develops a novel class of explainability algorithms for edge devices by fusing principles from robust statistics with sparse approximation techniques. The core innovation is a lightweight, adversarially-robust loss function that guides the generation of sparse feature attributions (e.g., saliency maps) that remain stable and faithful even when the model's inputs are perturbed. This enables real-time debugging and monitoring of on-device neural networks in security-critical or noisy real-world settings, where both computational resources are limited and explanations must be reliable. The result is a unified framework that provides trustworthy introspection for edge AI, addressing both resource constraints and vulnerability to adversarial manipulation simultaneously.

ideas.title: Robustness-Aware Policy Optimization: Identifying MDPs Where PPO's Heuristics Incur Minimal Robustness-Regret
ideas.description: This research direction challenges the belief in PPO's superiority by reframing the comparison with TRPO through the lens of robustness. Instead of comparing average performance, we propose to identify the characteristics of Markov Decision Processes (state transition dynamics, reward structure) where the sample complexity for learning a standard policy and a robust policy (e.g., to adversarial perturbations in observations or dynamics) is similar. For such 'minimal robustness-gap' MDPs, we can rigorously test if PPO's practical heuristics (clipping, early stopping) introduce a negligible performance deficit compared to TRPO's constrained updates, or if they inadvertently increase vulnerability to distributional shift. This reveals whether PPO's perceived efficiency comes at a hidden cost to robustness that is only exposed in certain environment classes.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Synthetic Data Generation via Domain Randomization
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of domain randomization from robotics simulation to the context of physics-based data augmentation for machine learning. In robotics, agents are trained in simulations where physical parameters like friction, lighting, and object mass are randomized to produce policies robust to real-world variance. Adapted here, this approach involves using a forward physics model not just to generate one accurate simulation, but to produce a vast array of synthetic training samples by systematically randomizing the model's physical parameters within realistic bounds. This method of physics-informed domain randomization generates a more informative and challenging dataset, encouraging the learning algorithm to develop decision boundaries that are invariant to nuisance physical variables and thus significantly more robust when deployed on real physical data.

ideas.title: Meta-Explanations for Novel Subpopulations in Sequential and Generative Models
ideas.description: This research direction develops a meta-learning framework for sparse linear explanation methods, enabling them to adapt quickly to novel subpopulations in sequential (e.g., RNNs, LSTMs) and generative models (e.g., GANs). The core idea is to treat the generation of a faithful, sparse explanation for a new, underrepresented data subgroup as a few-shot learning task. By meta-learning the explanation mechanism, the system can produce interpretable rationales for model predictions on novel subpopulations using only a handful of examples, without costly retraining. This synergy allows for both robust model debugging/trust in non-stationary environments and provides insights into how model behavior generalizes or fails across emerging data groups.

ideas.title: Extend framework to sequential and generative models
ideas.description: Apply the sparse linear explanation method to recurrent networks and generative models like GANs.
ideas.sourceTitle: Leveraging Sparse Linear Layers for Debuggable Deep Networks

ideas.title: Expand core scenarios and metrics
ideas.description: Continuously update the benchmark with new scenarios and metrics as models and societal needs evolve.
ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Minimal Perturbation for Targeted Phenotype Shift in Gene Regulatory Networks
ideas.description: This research seed is expanded via analogy to synthetic biology, where the goal is to achieve a desired phenotypic shift in an organism through the smallest possible genetic edit, preserving its original fitness. The analogous solution involves mapping the gene regulatory network, identifying key control points, and applying precise mutations. Adapted to the context of constrained model change, we propose treating a machine learning model (e.g., a large language model) as a computational network. The research would focus on algorithms to find the minimal parameter edit—a highly sparse, low-magnitude change to a critical subset of weights or activations—required to produce a specific behavioral shift (e.g., removing a bias, adding a capability). The core challenge is dual: scaling the precision of the edit to be minimal while scaling the robustness of the constraint that maximizes retention of the model's original functional profile across a broad distribution of inputs.

ideas.title: Control-Theoretic Robustness Continuum: Stabilizing Model Scaling via Perturbation Budget Scheduling
ideas.description: This research direction frames the standard learning objective as a point on a continuum defined by the adversarial perturbation budget. It then applies control theory to manage this budget as a dynamic state variable during the training of extremely large models. By designing controllers that schedule the perturbation budget—potentially increasing it to escape sharp minima early and annealing it to zero for final convergence—the approach actively stabilizes the training dynamics inherent to scaling. This yields a unified training paradigm where robustness is not just a final objective but a tunable control mechanism for achieving stable, scalable, and high-performance models.

ideas.title: Neurosymbolic Program Synthesis for Model-Agnostic, Faithful Explanations
ideas.description: This system generates rule-based explanations by synthesizing programs in a domain-specific language, but does not assume rules are encoded in sparse weights. Instead, it uses the target model as an oracle: candidate explanation programs are evaluated by how well their outputs match the model's predictions *and* how aligned their internal computational states are with the model's activation patterns via a learned correspondence. This ensures explanations are faithful to the model's actual reasoning, even for black-box or highly non-linear models, by treating program synthesis as an alignment search problem rather than a direct translation.

ideas.title: An Empirical Benchmarking Framework for Code-Level RL Optimizations
ideas.description: This project constructs a living benchmark that quantitatively evaluates the impact and interactions of code-level optimizations found in major RL implementations. Instead of merely listing techniques, it develops an automated pipeline to extract optimization patterns, create clean ablation studies, and run controlled experiments across standard environments like Atari, MuJoCo, and classic control tasks. The output is a continuously updatable database measuring each optimization's contribution to metrics such as final performance, sample efficiency, and training stability, providing actionable, evidence-based guidance for RL practitioners.

ideas.title: Ablation Cartography: A Formal Framework for Diagnosing Component-Specific Subpopulation Shift Hardness in Deep RL
ideas.description: This research formalizes a methodology for conducting ablation studies in deep RL that integrates training data cartography. The core innovation is to perform systematic ablations (e.g., removing specific network components, altering exploration strategies) not on the whole training set, but on data subsets identified by cartography as 'easy-to-learn' or 'hard-to-learn' for a target subpopulation shift. This creates a diagnostic matrix linking model components to data characteristics, revealing which components are critical for robustness on which types of data. The resulting framework provides a standardized, causal explanation for why certain shifts are hard for a given RL algorithm, moving beyond correlation to actionable architectural insights.

ideas.title: Scale to sequential decision tasks
ideas.description: Extend the sample complexity analysis to robust reinforcement learning or sequential prediction.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adversarially Robust Generalization Requires More Data

ideas.title: Architecture-Aware Neural Editing with Provable Guarantees
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using Neural Architecture Search (NAS) to discover optimal encoder-decoder or classifier architectures that are not only performant but also possess strong theoretical guarantees for post-hoc rule editing. The core synergy is to define the NAS search space and reward function to incorporate formal metrics of edit stability—such as bounds on output distortion or catastrophic forgetting—alongside accuracy. This would automate the creation of models whose architectural components (e.g., specific layer structures, activation functions, or connectivity patterns) are provably more amenable to safe, localized updates via direct rule rewriting. The outcome is a co-design of architecture and editing theory, moving beyond editing pre-existing arbitrary models to proactively constructing models designed for reliable and bounded modification.

ideas.title: Generating Interpretable Synthetic Data via Sparse Feature Induction
ideas.description: This research direction proposes generating synthetic text for pretraining where the generation process is guided by a dual objective: matching a target data distribution while also optimizing for the induction of sparse or grouped feature representations in the resulting model. The core experiment would train models on this engineered synthetic data and compare them to models trained on standard synthetic or real data, evaluating both task performance and human interpretability of the learned features. This allows for a direct test of whether sparsity causes interpretability by constructing data designed to cause it, moving beyond correlation. The ultimate goal is to develop data generation principles that yield models which are both high-performing and inherently more understandable.

ideas.title: Benchmark optimization contributions
ideas.description: Create a benchmark to quantify each optimization's contribution across diverse environments.
ideas.sourceTitle: Implementation Matters in Deep Policy Gradients: A Case Study on PPO and TRPO

ideas.title: Feature space sufficiency for all downstream tasks
ideas.description: Challenge that n-gram or simple features capture all aspects relevant for diverse future model capabilities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Data Selection for Language Models via Importance Resampling

ideas.title: Ecological Sampling for a Representative Atlas of RL Implementation Optimizations
ideas.description: This research applies ecological survey sampling techniques to the systematic study of code-level optimizations in reinforcement learning. Instead of exhaustively cataloging all possible optimizations from a limited set of known implementations, it treats the universe of RL code as an ecosystem to be sampled. Using stratified and adaptive sampling methods across diverse code repositories, it builds a statistically representative atlas that maps not only which optimizations exist, but also how frequently they are used, in what combinations they appear, and in which algorithmic or environmental contexts they are most fit. This enables data-driven insights into optimization efficacy and practical adoption patterns that a simple inventory or an unstructured scenario survey could not provide.

ideas.title: Neural Architecture Search for Sparse, Self-Explaining Codecs
ideas.description: This research fuses architecture search with sparse explanation to automate the design of encoder-decoder (codec) networks where the architecture itself is optimized to produce latent features that are maximally sparse and explanatory. The core innovation is to integrate sparsity and explanation quality metrics directly into the Neural Architecture Search (NAS) reward function, jointly searching for model structures that excel at accurate reconstruction while yielding latent spaces where a small subset of features provides a complete, human-understandable account of the input data. This moves beyond using NAS solely for compression performance, creating codecs whose internal representations are intrinsically designed for interpretability. The outcome would be a family of self-explaining models for domains like image or audio compression, where the compressed format is not just compact but also semantically structured for analysis.

ideas.title: Causal Feature-Editing via Learned Sparse Dictionaries
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond editing single hardware neurons to editing the underlying sparse, monosemantic features learned by a dictionary learning method applied to a model's activations. It identifies a small set of basis features responsible for a target behavior using causal tracing. The core edit is then applied to the weights that read out these specific features, followed by a lightweight fine-tuning phase with a consistency objective to preserve the model's performance on unrelated tasks. This ensures edits are both effective for the target concept and minimally disruptive to the network's other functions.

ideas.title: Core algorithm is primary performance driver
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that the core mathematical algorithm is the main source of performance gains.
ideas.sourceTitle: Implementation Matters in Deep Policy Gradients: A Case Study on PPO and TRPO

ideas.title: Extend analysis to broader model classes
ideas.description: Investigate the existence of non-robust features in architectures beyond standard convolutional networks.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adversarial Examples Are Not Bugs, They Are Features

ideas.title: Benchmark more models and versions
ideas.description: Evaluate more models, including new versions and those from other providers, under the standardized HELM framework.
ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Extend analysis to other algorithms
ideas.description: Apply the same case study methodology to other deep RL algorithms like DQN or SAC.
ideas.sourceTitle: Implementation Matters in Deep Policy Gradients: A Case Study on PPO and TRPO

ideas.title: A Model-Structure-Aware Hybrid Framework for Explaining Sequential and Generative Architectures
ideas.description: This research develops a new explanation framework specifically designed for the structural complexities of recurrent and generative models. It decomposes the explanation task into model-aware stages, applying locally faithful interpretability methods at key functional segments (e.g., per-time-step or per-layer) rather than forcing a single global linear model. A synthesis module then integrates these local explanations into a coherent global narrative that respects temporal causality for RNNs or the topology of the latent manifold for GANs. This approach ensures explanations are both accurate to the model's internal mechanics and comprehensible to a human analyst.

ideas.title: Scale to distributed RL systems
ideas.description: Study how code-level optimizations interact with and scale in distributed training setups.
ideas.sourceTitle: Implementation Matters in Deep Policy Gradients: A Case Study on PPO and TRPO

ideas.title: Scale up: compute-optimal robust learning
ideas.description: Given a fixed massive dataset, find the compute-optimal model size and training procedure for robustness.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adversarially Robust Generalization Requires More Data

ideas.title: HELM for small, specialized models
ideas.description: Apply the holistic evaluation framework to small, task-specific models, not just large general-purpose LMs.
ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Immunological Negative Selection for Model Debiasing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'negative selection' algorithm from computational immunology to debias machine learning models. In artificial immune systems, detectors are censored during training if they react to 'self' (normal) patterns, ensuring the immune response targets only foreign 'non-self' threats. Analogously, during the model training loop, we continuously generate sparse explanations (e.g., via SHAP or LIME) to identify input features the model is using for predictions that are known to be spurious or correlated with bias. These features are treated as 'self' patterns the model must learn to ignore. A regularization penalty is applied to any internal neuron, attention head, or representation that shows high activation correlation with these spurious explanation maps, effectively performing 'negative selection' on harmful feature detectors. This forces the model's capacity to re-allocate to learning robust, invariant features, integrating explanation-based feedback directly into the optimization process to build models inherently resistant to known biases and spurious correlations.

ideas.title: A Comparative Pharmacokinetics Framework for Deep RL Algorithm Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from pharmacology, where a standardized pharmacokinetic (ADME) framework is used to compare different drug compounds, to the context of deep RL algorithm analysis. The analogous solution involves applying a consistent battery of tests to different entities to understand their systemic behavior. Adapted, this means developing a standardized evaluation suite of core 'algorithmic pharmacokinetic' metrics and benchmark environments. This suite would then be systematically applied to other deep RL algorithms like DQN and SAC, using the same case study methodology, to generate comparable profiles that elucidate their intrinsic operational dynamics, strengths, and weaknesses in a unified manner.

ideas.title: Apply Progressive Stress Training for Adversarial Robustness
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of progressive stress hardening from materials science to improve adversarial robustness in machine learning. In metallurgy, a metal is strengthened ('work hardened') by subjecting it to gradually increasing mechanical stress, which creates a dense network of dislocations that impede future deformation, preventing immediate fracture. Analogously, for neural network training, we propose a 'Progressive Stress Training' curriculum where the strength and complexity of adversarial perturbations (e.g., the epsilon bound and iteration count of PGD attacks) are systematically increased according to a schedule over training epochs. This allows the model to first learn a robust baseline against weaker attacks, easing the initial learning problem, before its representations are progressively hardened against stronger, near-optimal adversarial examples, leading to a more fundamentally robust model architecture.

ideas.title: Adversarial Counterfactual Probes for Black-Box Model Editing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of directed evolution from evolutionary biology to the challenge of editing black-box models. In directed evolution, a biological function (e.g., enzyme activity) is modified by applying external selective pressure on a population through iterative cycles of mutation and selection, without requiring a complete internal blueprint. Analogously, we propose to edit a classifier's behavior by treating it as a 'phenotype' and applying selective pressure via its API. The method involves generating a population of diverse, minimal adversarial perturbations (analogous to mutations) to input data. By querying the model and selecting only those perturbations that produce the desired change in output (e.g., flipping a specific classification), we can iteratively evolve a set of 'editing vectors'. These vectors form an external counterfactual probe—a curated dataset or transformation function—that, when applied to future inputs, reliably alters the model's behavior in a targeted way, all without accessing internal weights, features, or gradients.

ideas.title: Inverse Spectroscopy: Synthesizing Inputs to Target Sparse Explanatory Features
ideas.description: This idea adapts the inverse problem from spectroscopy to the domain of machine learning interpretability. In spectroscopy, one often solves for the material that would produce a specific spectral fingerprint by synthesizing candidate samples and refining their composition. Analogously, here we aim to synthesize prototypical or counterfactual inputs that activate specific, sparse explanation features in a model (e.g., specific nodes in a concept bottleneck or features in a sparse autoencoder). The adapted solution involves an iterative 'synthesis-and-test' framework: define a target explanation feature as the 'signature' to match, use a generator to produce candidate inputs, measure the resulting explanation (feature activation), and optimize the generator via gradient-based or evolutionary methods to minimize the difference from the target signature. This enables controlled generation of inputs that elucidate the precise conditions under which specific explanatory features fire, scaling the inverse mapping from explanations to inputs.

ideas.title: Geological Stratigraphy for Neural Network Rule Extraction
ideas.description: This research adapts geological stratigraphy methods to the problem of generating human-readable rules from sparse neural network weights. In geology, scientists reconstruct complete historical layers and environmental rules from sparse core samples using principles of superposition and lateral continuity. Similarly, we treat sparse weight patterns in neural networks as 'core samples' through the decision space. By applying adapted continuity principles, we can reconstruct complete, hierarchical rule sets that explain how sparse activations combine to form decisions. This program synthesis approach generates symbolic, human-interpretable explanations that maintain fidelity to the original model's behavior while providing the transparency of rule-based systems.

ideas.title: Scale up: trade-off in large language models
ideas.description: Investigate if the robustness-accuracy trade-off manifests in massive-scale generative models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Robustness May Be at Odds with Accuracy

ideas.title: Certifiably Robust Models via Statistically Stable Training and Convex Certification
ideas.description: This approach combines the training-time stability of robust statistical losses with test-time certification to overcome the limitations of purely statistical defenses. The model is trained using a robust, non-convex loss function derived from M-estimation to reduce sensitivity to input variations and improve gradient alignment. For certification, the final network layer is designed to be convex with respect to the input, or the model is used within a randomized smoothing framework, enabling the computation of guaranteed robustness radii for each prediction. This results in a system that is both empirically harder to attack and provides verifiable security guarantees against bounded adversaries.

ideas.title: Concept-Bottlenecked Multimodal Generation for Interpretable and Controllable Synthesis
ideas.description: This research fuses concept bottleneck models (CBMs) with multimodal generative architectures to create systems that produce non-text outputs (like images or 3D structures) through a sparse, human-defined concept layer. The key innovation is using this interpretable concept bottleneck not just for classification, but as a control and debugging interface for the generative process. Researchers or users can inspect, intervene on, or correct specific concept activations (e.g., 'has wings,' 'is metallic') to directly steer and debug the model's generative reasoning, fixing errors in the synthesized output. This results in generative models whose internal reasoning is more transparent, controllable, and alignable with human intent than standard end-to-end approaches.

ideas.title: Physics-Augmented Sparse Pixel-Level Explanations (PASPLE)
ideas.description: This research direction combines sparse linear explainers applied directly to massive pixel spaces with physics-based data augmentation to create a novel framework for model interpretation and robustness testing. The core idea is to use physics simulators (e.g., for lighting, material, or motion) to generate augmented data samples that probe a model's decision boundaries in a physically plausible feature space. A sparse explainer then identifies a minimal set of raw pixels whose values, when perturbed according to these physical constraints, would flip the model's prediction. This yields explanations that are not only parsimonious and rooted in the high-dimensional input but are also inherently robust and meaningful, as they reflect possible real-world variations rather than adversarial noise.

ideas.title: Differentiable Inference-Time Projection for Consistent Constraint Enforcement
ideas.description: This approach introduces a trainable, lightweight projection module that sits atop a frozen, pre-trained model. During inference, the module takes the model's internal representations or logits and applies a minimal, differentiable correction to ensure the output satisfies specified fairness or robustness constraints. The projection is trained using a bi-objective loss that strictly enforces the constraints while heavily penalizing deviation from the original model's predictions, preserving accuracy. This ensures the model's core knowledge remains intact while providing guaranteed adherence to the target properties.

ideas.title: Dual: optimize for simplicity, not just reward
ideas.description: Search for implementations that maximize performance-per-complexity.
ideas.sourceTitle: Implementation Matters in Deep Policy Gradients: A Case Study on PPO and TRPO

ideas.title: Challenge the need for massive datasets
ideas.description: Explore if small, meticulously curated datasets yield more reliable progress than large, noisy benchmarks.
ideas.sourceTitle: From ImageNet to Image Classification: Contextualizing Progress on Benchmarks

ideas.title: Batch dependence is necessary for effect
ideas.description: Create a non-batch-dependent method that achieves similar gradient predictability.
ideas.sourceTitle: How Does Batch Normalization Help Optimization? (No, It Is Not About Internal Covariate Shift)

ideas.title: Influence-Guided Neural Architecture Search for Interpretable and Robust Codec Design
ideas.description: This research fuses influence function-based explainability with neural architecture search for codec design. The core idea is to use influence functions, which trace model predictions back to influential training samples, to identify architectural weaknesses and data-specific failure modes during the NAS process for encoder-decoder pairs. This diagnostic feedback is then used as an additional, interpretability-driven objective or constraint within the NAS optimization loop, steering the search away from architectures that are overly sensitive to small, noisy, or outlier training samples. The result is the automated co-design of a neural codec's architecture and its inherent explainability profile, producing models that are efficient, high-performing, and more transparent and robust by construction.

ideas.title: Create a micro-scale predictable performance testbed
ideas.description: Develop tiny models that reliably predict giant model behaviors for rapid experimentation.
ideas.sourceTitle: GPT-4 Technical Report

ideas.title: Compute-Optimal Scaling for Robust Feature Transfer
ideas.description: This research investigates how the compute-optimal model size and training procedure for robustness on a fixed massive dataset are fundamentally influenced by the transfer dynamics of both robust and non-robust features. By analyzing how these competing feature sets transfer between architectures of varying scales and across related tasks, the work aims to discover scaling laws that predict the optimal compute allocation for suppressing harmful non-robust transfer while maximizing beneficial robust feature generalization. The core novelty is a unified framework that treats feature transfer not just as a behavioral phenomenon, but as a central variable determining the efficiency frontier for building robust models.

ideas.title: Causal Ablation Framework for Deep RL: From Component Removal to Mechanism Attribution
ideas.description: This research develops a framework that replaces simple component-removal ablation with a principled methodology for causal mechanism attribution in deep RL agents. It requires the construction of a causal graph representing hypothesized interactions between agent components and environment factors, guiding a series of targeted interventions beyond deletion, such as component substitution, noise injection, and capability restriction. The framework standardizes the use of attribution methods (e.g., Shapley values) to quantify each component's marginal and interactive contribution to performance across a diverse set of environment benchmarks. The output is not just a performance table, but a standardized causal report that explains why an agent works, enabling stronger generalization predictions and more insightful algorithmic improvements.

ideas.title: Learned features are optimal for sparse explanation
ideas.description: Investigate if jointly learning features for sparsity and accuracy improves explanations.
ideas.sourceTitle: Leveraging Sparse Linear Layers for Debuggable Deep Networks

ideas.title: Tight Sample Complexity Bounds for Robust Reinforcement Learning with Structured Dynamics Adversaries
ideas.description: This research provides a rigorous, non-asymptotic sample complexity analysis for robust reinforcement learning under a precisely defined class of structured adversaries affecting the environment's transition dynamics. It moves beyond worst-case, unstructured uncertainty sets by modeling the adversary's power via constraints like bounded total variation or smooth perturbations over the state-action space. The core technical contribution is a decomposition theorem separating the sample cost of learning the nominal model from the premium required for robustness, leading to instance-dependent bounds that are significantly tighter than generic worst-case analyses. These results directly yield practical insights for algorithm design, such as optimal exploration strategies for robust RL and conditions under which robust methods provide sample-efficient generalization to perturbed environments.

ideas.title: Optimize DSIR for post-training or fine-tuning data
ideas.description: Use DSIR to select high-impact examples for supervised fine-tuning or reinforcement learning from human feedback.
ideas.sourceTitle: Data Selection for Language Models via Importance Resampling

ideas.title: Expand the range of predictable scaling behaviors
ideas.description: Extend scaling prediction methods to cover a wider set of model behaviors and metrics.
ideas.sourceTitle: GPT-4 Technical Report

ideas.title: Adaptive Optical Tweezers for Single-Neuron Micro-Editing
ideas.description: This idea refines the technique for making ultra-localized edits to individual neurons or tiny subnetworks by drawing an analogy from biophysics. The analogous field is cellular manipulation, where optical tweezers use adaptive, focused laser beams to manipulate microscopic targets within a cell without collateral damage. The analogous solution involves using corrective optics to achieve nanometer-scale precision. Adapted to this AI research context, the solution is to create 'adaptive gradient tweezers.' This technique would generate a dynamically focused and corrected optimization signal (a micro-gradient) that can be applied with extreme precision to the parameters of a single artificial neuron or a micro-synapse. By using real-time feedback to account for and nullify the diffusive effects of the surrounding network's activity, the method ensures the edit is confined to the intended target, enabling surgical-scale modifications within massive neural networks without perturbing their overall function—a true scaling down of the editing process.

ideas.title: Compute-Optimal Robust Learning via Metabolic Scaling Laws
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from Metabolic Scaling Theory in ecology to the problem of compute-optimal robust learning. Given a fixed massive dataset (analogous to a stable ecosystem with finite resources), the goal is to find the optimal model size and corresponding training procedure (computational budget) for robustness. Drawing from Kleiber's Law, which defines an optimal scaling between body size and metabolic rate, we propose to empirically derive a 'Robustness Scaling Law.' This law would describe the relationship between model parameter count (N) and the optimal training compute (C) required to maximize a robustness metric on the fixed dataset, expressed as C_opt ~ k * N^α. The core research involves running a large-scale scaling experiment to fit the exponent (α) and constant (k), thereby identifying the compute-optimal model size and training FLOPs for robust performance, defining an efficient frontier for scaling decisions.

ideas.title: Relax perturbation model assumptions
ideas.description: Investigate the trade-off under different adversarial threat models beyond bounded perturbations.
ideas.sourceTitle: Robustness May Be at Odds with Accuracy

ideas.title: Invert the crowd-sourcing fidelity goal
ideas.description: Instead of minimizing noise, maximize the learnable signal from noisy annotations to understand model robustness.
ideas.sourceTitle: From ImageNet to Image Classification: Contextualizing Progress on Benchmarks

ideas.title: Semantic Dataset Editing for Non-Linear Feature Erasure
ideas.description: This research direction combines dataset filtering with model editing to proactively shape model capabilities. We use a small, edited 'probe model' (where a complex, non-linearly separable feature has been erased or altered) to compute semantic feature representations for candidate training examples. These representations then guide the Data Selection with Importance Resampling (DSIR) process to down-weight or filter out data points that strongly activate the target entangled feature in the probe model. This creates a curated dataset that, when used to train a new model from scratch, results in a model that never learned the problematic feature, avoiding the need for difficult post-hoc editing on entangled representations.

ideas.title: Robust minimal edit for constrained model change with adversarial validation
ideas.description: This research develops a method to find the most efficient edit for a desired behavioral shift while guaranteeing robustness. Instead of seeking only the smallest change, it proactively identifies and defends against potential failure modes introduced by the edit through adversarial simulation and testing. The final edit is the one that satisfies the behavioral constraint while maximizing retained performance on a broad, adversarially-validated set of functions and inputs. This produces safer, more reliable model adjustments that are resistant to unintended consequences and exploitation.

ideas.title: Sparse Linear Models as Sufficient Explanations: An Analogy from Neuroscience's Sparse Coding Hypothesis
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the assumption that explaining complex, non-linear models requires equally complex explanation structures. Drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience's Sparse Coding Hypothesis—which showed complex visual inputs could be represented by sparse linear combinations of basis functions—we propose that model explanations might also be fundamentally sparse and linear. We adapt this solution by hypothesizing that for a given complex model, there exists a learned, overcomplete dictionary of interpretable 'explanation prototypes' in some transformed space (e.g., an embedding space or a space of concept activations). The explanation for any individual prediction is then the minimal set (sparse linear combination) of these prototypes required to reconstruct the model's reasoning path or decision boundary locally. The core research explores whether such sparse linear approximations are not just viable but sufficient for human-interpretable explanation, potentially offering greater simplicity, stability, and computational efficiency than complex, non-linear explanation methods.

ideas.title: Extend provable trade-off to complex models
ideas.description: Prove the accuracy-robustness trade-off exists in more complex, realistic model architectures.
ideas.sourceTitle: Robustness May Be at Odds with Accuracy

ideas.title: Physics-Guided Augmentation for Targeted Model Editing in Entangled Spaces
