ideas.description: This research direction proposes using physics-based generative models to create targeted data augmentations specifically designed to disentangle a non-linearly separable target feature within a neural network's representation space. By simulating counterfactual scenarios governed by physical laws, we can generate novel samples that systematically vary the target feature while holding confounding factors constant, effectively 'carving out' a more linear subspace for that feature. This artificially induced separability then enables the precise application of model editing techniques, such as rank-one updates, to correct or insert knowledge. The synergy transforms model editing from a passive procedure applied to existing representations into an active one that first shapes the geometry of the representation to make editing feasible and reliable.

ideas.title: Explanation-Guided, Scalable Model Editing for Foundational Model Correction
ideas.description: This research direction combines the scalability of direct model editing with the diagnostic power of sparse explanations to create a new paradigm for correcting foundational models. We develop methods to first generate sparse explanations that identify the specific model parameters (e.g., attention heads, MLP neurons) responsible for a model's reliance on spurious or biased features. These explanations then guide a targeted parameter-editing procedure, such as a constrained rank-one update, to directly rewrite the problematic prediction rules while minimally affecting unrelated capabilities. This results in a post-hoc, explanation-aware editing framework that can systematically debias or robustify very large models with surgical precision, a capability beyond standalone editing or explanation-based training.

ideas.title: The Oracle as a Metabolic Precursor: Characterizing Learning Efficiency with Robust Feature Injection
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by drawing an analogy from metabolic engineering. In that field, supplying key metabolic precursors externally bypasses inefficient biosynthesis, shifting the system's bottlenecks and enabling new optimization strategies. Similarly, assuming access to a robust feature oracle provides 'intellectual precursors' to a learning algorithm, fundamentally changing the sample complexity dynamics. The adapted study will model the oracle's output as a controlled resource (akin to a precursor feed) and analyze learning in this regime. The core objective is to develop a theoretical framework that quantifies the new sample complexity landscape, identifying what constitutes the 'rate-limiting step' in learning—such as the complexity of the head/combiner function or resilience to adversarial perturbations in the subsequent layers—when robust features are no longer the primary cost. This shifts the assumption challenge from merely having the oracle to understanding and characterizing the new efficiency frontiers and bottlenecks it creates.

ideas.title: Extend evaluations to all GPT-5 system models
ideas.description: Future work includes detailed evaluations for models beyond gpt-5-main and gpt-5-thinking, as mentioned in the appendix.

ideas.title: Benchmark more advanced robustness interventions
ideas.description: Systematically evaluate newer or more sophisticated robustness training techniques using the provided BREEDS benchmark suites.
ideas.sourceTitle: BREEDS: Benchmarks for Subpopulation Shift

ideas.title: Architecture-Agnostic Data Editing for Black-Box Model Correction
ideas.description: This research direction proposes that for a given behavioral edit (e.g., correcting a bias, updating a fact), there exists an optimal, minimal set of input-output examples whose effectiveness is largely independent of the underlying model's architecture and training details. The core challenge is to algorithmically discover this universal corrective dataset using only query access to a diverse set of static, black-box models (with varying architectures and optimizers) as testbeds. Success would yield a general procedure for editing black-box models via their API by simply injecting this pre-computed, architecture-agnostic data into their input stream or fine-tuning pipeline, bypassing the need for internal access or model-specific adaptation.

ideas.title: Design new methods based on smoothness
ideas.description: Create novel optimization techniques that explicitly enforce gradient predictive stability and smooth landscapes.
ideas.sourceTitle: How Does Batch Normalization Help Optimization? (No, It Is Not About Internal Covariate Shift)

ideas.title: Apply bandit algorithms for online data selection
ideas.description: Treat different data sources or selection policies as arms to optimize for downstream performance.
ideas.sourceTitle: Data Selection for Language Models via Importance Resampling

ideas.title: Create ultra-sparse global model explanations via compressed sensing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from compressed sensing in signal processing to the problem of creating ultra-sparse global model explanations. In compressed sensing, a sparse signal is perfectly reconstructed from very few measurements by solving an L1-minimization problem. Analogously, we treat the complete, interpretable logic of a machine learning model as a high-dimensional but sparse 'explanation signal.' By designing a minimal set of global, linear probes (model outputs or gradients across a synthesized dataset) and formulating an L1-regularized optimization, we can recover a single, ultra-sparse set of features and their weights that best reconstruct the model's overall decision behavior for all classes simultaneously. This provides a highly compact, global explanation, scaling efficiently with model complexity by exploiting the inherent sparsity in explanatory factors.

ideas.title: Automated Causal Localization and Intervention for Model Behavior Correction
ideas.description: This research develops methods to automatically localize the computational sub-processes within a neural network responsible for specific, undesired behaviors and to propose minimal, interpretable interventions for correction. Instead of searching for human-like 'rules,' the system uses causal mediation analysis and gradient-based attribution to identify critical neurons, attention heads, or feature directions whose activation patterns are necessary and sufficient for the problematic behavior. It then generates candidate edits—such as patching activations, adjusting projection weights, or adding small steering vectors—and validates them through rigorous counterfactual testing to ensure they correct the target behavior without damaging unrelated capabilities. The output is a set of verifiable, localized intervention proposals that directly address the root computational causes of model failures.

ideas.title: Inverse: use robust features for data synthesis
ideas.description: Use the human-aligned features of robust models to generate more naturalistic synthetic data.
ideas.sourceTitle: Robustness May Be at Odds with Accuracy

ideas.title: Inverse Editing via Compressed Behavioral Codes
ideas.description: This research direction proposes training a generative model to learn a compressed, disentangled representation of a base model's behavior across tasks. The core innovation is to then frame the inverse problem—recovering edits from observed behavioral changes—as decoding within this compressed behavioral code space. By using the compression objective during training, the learned codes naturally correspond to fundamental, composable rule edits, making the inversion more robust and interpretable. This enables not only the identification of what edits were made but also the synthesis of new, minimal edits to steer model behavior in desired directions.

ideas.title: Multi-Task Sufficiency: Sample Complexity for Task-Agnostic Robust Representation Learning
ideas.description: This work formalizes and studies the sample complexity of learning feature representations that are provably sufficient for a broad family of downstream classification tasks while being invariant to specified nuisance variations. The core innovation is a framework that defines representational robustness not in isolation, but through its simultaneous utility and stability across a constrained task manifold. The analysis will derive fundamental bounds on the data required to learn such representations, explicitly linking the breadth of the task family, the complexity of the nuisance model, and the dimensionality of the representation to sample efficiency.

ideas.title: Causally-Constrained, Realistic Input Synthesis for Robust Feature Explanation
ideas.description: This redesigned system synthesizes inputs to activate specific explanation features under a dual constraint. First, it uses a pre-trained generative model (e.g., a GAN or diffusion model) as a strong prior to ensure outputs are realistic and within the data manifold. Second, it defines the target not as activation in a single explanation method, but as consistent activation across a diverse set of sparse explainers (e.g., different saliency maps, concept activation vectors). This cross-method consistency acts as a proxy for causal relevance, filtering out method-specific artifacts. The final output is a set of realistic prototypes or counterfactuals that robustly and meaningfully correspond to the explanatory feature.

ideas.title: Invert the scaling priority for efficiency
ideas.description: Pursue models that match GPT-4's capabilities with drastically less compute, not more.
ideas.sourceTitle: GPT-4 Technical Report

ideas.title: Enhance router training with more real-time signals
ideas.description: Continuously train the router on additional real user signals beyond switching, preferences, and correctness.
ideas.sourceTitle: OpenAI GPT-5 System Card

ideas.title: Theoretical Guarantees for Model Editing via Control-Theoretic Stability Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts the concept of Lyapunov stability from control theory to provide formal guarantees for direct classifier editing. In control theory, one proves a system remains stable after a perturbation by showing a chosen Lyapunov function decays. Analogously, we propose to define a formal metric (a combination of loss on retained knowledge and the new edited rule) for a neural network. After a direct parameter edit (the perturbation), we would establish theoretical conditions under which this metric is bounded, thereby proving the edit is both effective (the new rule is learned) and stable (performance on unrelated tasks does not catastrophically degrade). This framework aims to move empirical editing results towards a theory with provable bounds on post-edit performance and stability.

ideas.title: Sparse, Interpretable Features via a Rate-Distortion-Sparsity Trade-off
ideas.description: This research investigates if jointly learning features for sparsity and accuracy improves explanations by drawing an analogy from information-theoretic signal compression. In lossy compression, optimal codecs are designed by solving a joint rate-distortion-perception optimization, balancing bit-rate (sparsity), reconstruction accuracy, and perceptual quality. We adapt this solution by formulating feature learning as optimizing a combined loss: Task Loss (Distortion) + λ * Sparsity Regularization (Rate) + μ * Explanation Fidelity Loss (Perception). The Explanation Fidelity Loss would be defined as the error between the model's predictions and the predictions of a sparse, interpretable proxy model (e.g., a linear model with L1 penalty) applied to the learned features. This framework challenges the assumption that features learned purely for accuracy are suitable for post-hoc explanation, proposing instead that features must be learned under an explicit trade-off where sparsity and inherent explainability are direct components of the training objective, leading to optimally sparse explanatory features.

ideas.title: Use generative models only at the encoder
ideas.description: Invert the proposed architecture by placing the generative model in the encoder, not the decoder.
ideas.sourceTitle: Generative Compression

ideas.title: Influence-Guided Active Learning for Robust and Explainable Model Debugging
ideas.description: This research direction integrates active learning with influence functions to create a unified framework for robust model development and debugging. The core idea is to use training data influence scores to design a novel active learning acquisition function that prioritizes samples likely to reduce robust generalization error—such as those near adversarial decision boundaries or from underrepresented subpopulations. Simultaneously, the sparse explanations generated for these queried samples provide immediate, interpretable feedback for debugging the model's failure modes. This creates a synergistic cycle where data collection directly targets the model's weaknesses as revealed by its explanations, leading to more efficient robustness improvements and inherently more explainable models.

ideas.title: Dual: Evaluate the evaluators
ideas.description: Systematically analyze the limitations, biases, and costs of the evaluation metrics and scenarios themselves.
ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Use data augmentation from physics models
ideas.description: Incorporate physics-based data augmentation to generate more informative samples for learning robust decision boundaries.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adversarially Robust Generalization Requires More Data

ideas.title: Prioritize encoding speed over decoding speed
ideas.description: Flip the computational focus to make the encoder complex and the decoder very simple.
ideas.sourceTitle: Generative Compression

ideas.title: Generating Adversarial and Prototypical Text from Sparse Explanations
ideas.description: This research direction combines the inverse problem of generating inputs from explanations with the search for non-robust features in NLP. The core methodology involves using sparse explanation techniques (e.g., from models like Sparse Autoencoders in SAEs) on language models to identify specific, potentially non-robust features. Then, we actively synthesize prototypical or counterfactual text sequences designed to maximally activate or ablate these individual features. This synthesis acts as a powerful tool to characterize the causal role of these features, systematically generate new adversarial examples, and create interpretable prototypes that reveal the model's latent concepts, thereby bridging feature discovery and causal intervention in language understanding.

ideas.title: Post-Algorithmic Constraint Enforcement: Performance as a Function of Fairness and Robustness Edits
ideas.description: This research challenges the primacy of the core learning algorithm by proposing that a model's ultimate performance profile is predominantly determined by the systematic application of direct, post-training edits for fairness and robustness. Instead of viewing these edits as mere corrective patches that degrade accuracy, the approach re-frames them as the primary performance levers, where different editing strategies and rule-sets directly dictate the trade-off surface between accuracy, fairness, and robustness. The novel capability is the ability to design and optimize over the space of constraint-enforcing edits themselves to *engineer* desired performance characteristics, making the editing protocol, not the base model's architecture or original training, the central object of study and optimization.

ideas.title: Compose global explanations from a sparse dictionary of verified local primitives
ideas.description: This approach first distills the complex model's behavior into a sparse set of simple, high-fidelity local explanations (primitives) valid within specific data subspaces. A lightweight selector model then learns to combine these primitives to explain global behavior, producing class-specific and region-specific explanations without forcing a single monolithic rule. This ensures both global coverage and local fidelity, overcoming the oversimplification of a one-size-fits-all sparse model.

ideas.title: Surgical Gene Editing for Large Foundation Models
ideas.description: This idea scales the direct rewriting of prediction rules in large pre-trained foundation models by drawing an analogy from synthetic biology. The analogous field is Synthetic Biology and Genome Engineering, where solutions like CRISPR-Cas systems allow for precise, targeted gene edits using a guide mechanism and a specialized enzyme, enabling scalable rewriting of an organism's blueprint. The adapted research idea, 'Surgical Gene Editing for Large Foundation Models,' proposes designing a neural 'guide network' to locate sparse, responsible subnetworks for a specific rule. A compact 'editor module' is then surgically integrated at this site to directly overwrite the rule, analogous to a CRISPR payload, allowing for efficient, large-scale model editing with minimal collateral impact on the rest of the model's knowledge.

ideas.title: Scale down: Create micro-benchmarks
ideas.description: Build tiny, perfectly curated datasets to serve as a ground-truth test for model understanding.
ideas.sourceTitle: From ImageNet to Image Classification: Contextualizing Progress on Benchmarks

ideas.title: Interactive Debugging of Online Sparse Models with Single-Sample Adaptive Normalization
ideas.description: This research fuses interactive machine learning for debugging with single-sample online normalization to create a system for debugging models in non-stationary data streams. A user can interactively correct a sparse online model's predictions or explanations on individual arriving samples, triggering immediate retraining. The core innovation is a normalization layer that dynamically stabilizes feature statistics for each single sample in real-time, ensuring that user corrections and subsequent model updates are effective and do not cause catastrophic divergence. This creates a stable, interactive debugging loop for streaming AI systems where traditional batch normalization is impossible.

ideas.title: Quantifying Trust in Sparse Explanations via Psychometric Scaling and Signal Detection Theory
ideas.description: This research adapts methodologies from psychophysics and sensory psychology to rigorously quantify human trust in sparse AI explanations. The analogous problem is measuring unobservable subjective perceptions like brightness. That field solves it by using psychometric scaling to create quantitative perceptual scales and Signal Detection Theory (SDT) to disentangle perceptual sensitivity from decision bias. Adapted here, we propose large-scale human studies where participants evaluate model predictions with sparse explanations. First, psychometric methods (e.g., magnitude estimation) will be used to construct a scale of 'perceived trustworthiness' based on explanation sparsity parameters. Second, in a controlled task (e.g., debugging or accepting predictions), SDT will be applied. This analysis will yield two key metrics: the user's 'sensitivity' (d') to the actual usefulness of the explanation content, and their 'response bias' (criterion) towards trusting AI. This adaptation provides a robust, bias-corrected measure of how explanation sparsity truly affects trust and debugging efficiency, moving beyond simple self-reported ratings.

ideas.title: Inverse Behavioral Inference via Differential System Identification
ideas.description: This research adapts 'differential system identification' from control systems engineering to the AI alignment problem. In control theory, engineers infer parameter changes in physical systems by observing behavioral differences and solving an inverse optimization. Similarly, we propose treating an AI model as a system whose behavior is governed by internal 'rules' (parameters, architectures, or learned constraints). Given an original model and its behaviorally altered version, the method would use a curated set of behavioral probes (input-output pairs) to define a discrepancy metric. An optimization framework would then search the space of possible rule edits (e.g., changes to specific model components, fine-tuning gradients, or prompt-based constraints) to find the minimal set most likely to produce the observed behavioral change. This provides a principled, scalable approach to audit model modifications, diagnose unintended side effects of edits, and improve interpretability of model interventions.

ideas.title: Dual: Generate synthetic biased datasets
ideas.description: Create controlled synthetic datasets with known artifacts to study model exploitation mechanisms.
ideas.sourceTitle: From ImageNet to Image Classification: Contextualizing Progress on Benchmarks

ideas.title: Causal Ablation as Primary Contribution: A Hypothesis-Driven Framework for RL Analysis
ideas.description: This framework repositions ablation studies as the principal vehicle for scientific explanation in reinforcement learning research. Instead of presenting a novel algorithm with supporting ablations, a paper's core contribution becomes a falsifiable causal model explaining why and how certain components affect performance in a domain. The experimental narrative is built around strategically designed intervention studies that test specific causal pathways within this model. This approach mandates that papers provide a generalized explanatory insight, moving beyond incremental component testing to deliver a fundamental understanding of algorithmic mechanisms. The result is research that prioritizes robust, transferable knowledge over isolated performance benchmarks.

ideas.title: Quantify human perception alignment benefit
ideas.description: Empirically measure how robust model representations better align with human perception.
ideas.sourceTitle: Robustness May Be at Odds with Accuracy

ideas.title: Improve multimodal reasoning beyond text output

ideas.sourceTitle: GPT-4 Technical Report

ideas.title: Amortized Influence Sketching for Stable, Real-Time Model Debugging
ideas.description: This system addresses the computational and instability flaws of naive influence functions by embedding influence tracking directly into the optimization process. During training, it maintains a dynamic, sparse sketch of parameter-data interactions using efficient hashing and incremental updates. This allows for real-time retrieval of robust influence estimates for any prediction, which are then fused with contemporary sparse feature attributions. The result is a practical, scalable tool for debugging model decisions that links them to both influential training examples and salient input features with guaranteed stability.

ideas.title: Challenge next-token prediction as the sole pre-training objective
ideas.description: Explore alternative or auxiliary pre-training objectives beyond autoregressive next-token prediction.

ideas.title: Causal Attribution Benchmark for Optimization Techniques
ideas.description: This benchmark introduces a causal experimental framework to isolate and quantify the true contribution of an optimization algorithm, separating it from its interactions with environmental variables. It employs a full-factorial design that systematically varies model architectures, dataset families, and hyperparameter baselines to measure the optimization's direct effect and its higher-order interactions. The output is a decomposed performance profile, attributing gains or losses to the optimization's core mechanism versus its context-specific compatibility, providing robust, generalizable insight into when and why an optimization works.

ideas.title: Edit rules for fairness or robustness constraints
ideas.description: Use direct rewriting to enforce specific fairness criteria or adversarial robustness properties on a trained model.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editing a classifier by rewriting its prediction rules

ideas.title: Edit large-scale foundation models
ideas.description: Scale the direct rewriting methodology to edit prediction rules in very large, pre-trained foundation models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editing a classifier by rewriting its prediction rules

ideas.title: Dual problem: maximize accuracy given robustness
ideas.description: Formulate and solve the dual optimization: fix a robustness level and maximize standard accuracy.
ideas.sourceTitle: Robustness May Be at Odds with Accuracy

ideas.title: Use neural architecture search for codec design
ideas.description: Automate the discovery of optimal neural network architectures for the encoder-decoder pair.

ideas.title: Investigate the inverse: compression for generative models
ideas.description: Study how to best compress the parameters of the generative model itself.
ideas.sourceTitle: Generative Compression

ideas.title: Self-Supervised Discovery and Nullification of Latent Spurious Directions
ideas.description: This method automatically discovers spurious feature directions in a model's latent space without prior knowledge. It uses a contrastive learning setup on a small, varied dataset to find a candidate direction that, when edited, maximally disrupts a proxy for the spurious correlation (e.g., prediction on a biased subset) while minimally affecting a primary task's accuracy. The approach includes a robustness loop that iteratively refines the direction and validates its effectiveness by measuring generalization improvement on a carefully constructed out-of-distribution benchmark, ensuring the edit removes the true spurious feature without introducing new artifacts.

ideas.title: Assume models should not solve benchmarks
ideas.description: Design evaluation where a model exploiting dataset artifacts is penalized, not rewarded.
ideas.sourceTitle: From ImageNet to Image Classification: Contextualizing Progress on Benchmarks

ideas.title: Psychophysical Post-Hoc Debugging: Validating Explanations via Human Perceptual Distances
ideas.description: This research challenges the necessity of intrinsic interpretability by proposing that post-hoc explanations are sufficient for debugging, provided they can be validated against a formal, human-centric standard. It leverages psychophysical models to define 'perceptual explanation distances'—quantifiable measures of how differently a human would perceive the system's behavior versus the explanation's description of it. The core thesis is that a system is debuggable when, for any failure, a post-hoc explanation exists within a bounded perceptual distance from the true mechanistic cause, as defined by human perceptual thresholds. This creates a testable alignment condition for AI safety, where robustness is measured by the system's ability to generate explanations that are perceptually indistinguishable from the truth to a human debugger.

ideas.title: Architecture-Agnostic Influence Analysis for Non-Robust Feature Provenance
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using influence functions to perform causal tracing of non-robust features across a broad spectrum of model classes, including transformers, graph neural networks, and recurrent networks. By adapting influence estimation techniques to these diverse architectures, the work aims to identify which specific training examples are most responsible for instilling brittle, non-robust feature representations that harm out-of-distribution generalization. This synergy moves beyond merely cataloging the existence of such features to diagnosing their root cause in the training data, irrespective of architectural specifics. The ultimate goal is to provide a unified framework for auditing and editing models by surgically removing the influence of problematic data points, thereby improving robustness in a targeted, data-efficient manner.

ideas.title: Performance as an Emergent Property of System-Level Orchestration
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the core mathematical algorithm is the main source of performance gains by drawing an analogy from systems biology. In that field, a cell's function emerged not from the raw power of individual enzymes but from the system-level orchestration of their interactions. Adapting this solution, the research posits that computational performance is similarly an emergent property of system-level orchestration. The adapted idea shifts focus from the isolated core algorithm to the orchestration layer encompassing data movement, memory hierarchy utilization, task scheduling, and compiler strategies. By treating the algorithm as a component within a complex computational system, the primary performance driver becomes the optimization of the higher-order interactions and resource coordination surrounding it, rather than the algorithm's intrinsic mathematical efficiency alone.

ideas.title: Ensemble Editing via Coordinated Parameter Steering
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from synthetic biology, where metabolic pathways are coordinately rewired for a desired output. The analogous problem is optimizing a microbial cell factory by simultaneously editing multiple gene clusters to balance metabolic flux. The solution uses multiomic models to guide coordinated genetic perturbations. Adapted to machine learning, we treat an ensemble of classifiers as a prediction-generating network. We develop an algorithm that applies constrained, coordinated parameter updates ('steering vectors' or targeted rewrites) to all models in the ensemble at once. The goal is to efficiently edit the ensemble's joint behavior to satisfy a new global constraint (e.g., demographic parity, adversarial robustness) while maintaining the ensemble's core predictive performance and diversity, analogous to maintaining cellular viability while shifting chemical production.

ideas.title: Study inverse scaling: when bigger models perform worse
ideas.description: Systematically identify and analyze tasks where performance degrades with increased model scale.
ideas.sourceTitle: GPT-4 Technical Report

ideas.title: Level up performance in more common use cases
ideas.description: Extend the leveled-up performance improvements beyond writing, coding, and health to other common user tasks.

ideas.description: Develop efficient BatchNorm for extremely large batch sizes where statistics are unstable.

ideas.title: Improve efficiency of holistic evaluation
ideas.description: Develop methods to make comprehensive, multi-scenario evaluation more computationally efficient and scalable.
ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Holistic Evaluation and Editing of Specialized Models for Unknown Spurious Correlations
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the principles of holistic evaluation (HELM) with advanced model editing to address a critical gap in specialized model development. It proposes adapting the HELM framework to systematically probe small, task-specific models across diverse scenarios and demographics, not for benchmarking, but to automatically identify and characterize unknown spurious correlations they may rely on. Once these problematic features are surfaced through evaluation, the project develops and applies 'blind' editing techniques that can modify the model's internal representations to reduce dependence on these discovered spurious features, even without a known vector direction for them. This creates a closed-loop system of evaluation-driven editing specifically for the unique architectures and failure modes of specialized models, moving beyond large LMs.

ideas.title: Design robust training for noisy labels
ideas.description: Create training methods that are less sensitive to systematic label noise from dataset creation artifacts.
ideas.sourceTitle: From ImageNet to Image Classification: Contextualizing Progress on Benchmarks

ideas.title: Robust Interactive Debugging with Feedback Validation and User Modeling
ideas.description: This system enables interactive debugging of machine learning models by letting users refine explanations and retrain sparse models, but it adds a critical safeguard. It actively models the user's reliability and the consistency of their feedback over time to filter out noisy or contradictory corrections. Before committing to retraining, it simulates the proposed changes and can engage the user in a clarifying dialogue using counterfactuals. This ensures the iterative loop converges on genuinely improved model behavior rather than amplifying user errors.

ideas.title: Robust Feature Editing via Oracle-Guided Discovery
ideas.description: This research direction studies how to edit a model's representations to remove unknown spurious correlations when we only have access to an oracle that provides robust, high-level feature activations for inputs. The core challenge is to use the oracle's robust features as a stable reference point to identify the model's own learned—but spurious—feature directions within its latent space, without prior knowledge of what those spurious directions are. The synergy allows for sample-efficient learning of editing transformations that project out the discovered spurious components, aligning the model's representations with the oracle's robust features. This creates a powerful paradigm where model editing and robustness improvement are achieved through interaction with a feature oracle, rather than through extensive data collection or explicit specification of biases.

ideas.title: A Contribution Decomposition Benchmark via Isolability and Additivity Testing
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from pharmacology for quantifying drug combination effects to the problem of benchmarking optimization contributions in AI. The analogous field is Pharmacology/Drug Combination Studies, where solutions like isobolographic analysis isolate individual drug effects from synergistic or antagonistic interactions. The adapted idea is to create a benchmark suite that systematically tests AI optimizations (architectural changes, training tricks, regularization methods) both in isolation and in controlled combinations across diverse environments (e.g., different datasets, network backbones). By applying a formal decomposition model inspired by pharmacological interaction analysis, the benchmark will output quantified metrics for each optimization's baseline contribution, its consistent additive value, and its role in positive or negative interactions with other optimizations, providing a clear, interpretable map of how optimizations combine to produce final performance.

ideas.title: Single-sample online normalization
ideas.description: Create a normalization method for online/streaming learning with batch size of one.
ideas.sourceTitle: How Does Batch Normalization Help Optimization? (No, It Is Not About Internal Covariate Shift)

ideas.title: Self-Correcting Explanation Ensembles for Robust Feature Penalization
ideas.description: This system integrates an ensemble of diverse explanation methods into the training loop to debias models by penalizing spurious features. It uses consensus across explanation techniques to more reliably identify spurious correlations, reducing reliance on any single, potentially flawed, explanation. A meta-validation loop continuously assesses whether penalizing the identified features harms legitimate predictive performance on a clean hold-out set, allowing the system to self-correct. This creates a robust, adaptive training regime that minimizes the risk of penalizing useful features based on erroneous explanations.

ideas.title: Robust and non-robust features are separable
ideas.description: Investigate if robust and non-robust features can be perfectly disentangled within a model's representations.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adversarial Examples Are Not Bugs, They Are Features

ideas.title: Create ultra-sparse global model explanations
ideas.description: Fit a single sparse model explaining overall model behavior across all classes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Leveraging Sparse Linear Layers for Debuggable Deep Networks

ideas.title: Demographically-Calibrated Sparse Explanations for Language Models
ideas.description: This research investigates whether sparse linear approximations of complex language models can be calibrated using survey raking and post-stratification techniques to produce explanations that are faithful to the model's mechanics while also being statistically representative of a target population's demographic margins. The core synergy is the development of 'calibrated explanations,' where the sparse feature weights (e.g., for tokens or concepts) are adjusted to ensure the distribution of explanation importance scores aligns with pre-specified demographic benchmarks, correcting for sampling or latent bias in the explanatory features. This allows for the auditing of not just what a model predicts, but whether the reasoning it exposes varies systematically—and potentially unfairly—across groups, moving beyond predictive fairness to explanatory fairness.

ideas.title: Scale to non-visual domains like NLP
ideas.description: Search for and characterize analogous non-robust features in natural language processing tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adversarial Examples Are Not Bugs, They Are Features

ideas.title: Apply dynamical systems stability analysis

ideas.title: Systematically catalog code-level optimizations
ideas.description: Create a comprehensive inventory of code-level optimizations across major RL implementations.
ideas.sourceTitle: Implementation Matters in Deep Policy Gradients: A Case Study on PPO and TRPO

ideas.title: Robust Feature Oracle Distillation with Meta-Learned Adaptive Alignment
ideas.description: This research investigates learning not just from a robust feature oracle, but *how* to learn from it. The core problem is that oracle features, while robust, may be suboptimal or biased for the downstream task. The proposed system uses the oracle to bootstrap a diverse ensemble of initial models, then applies a meta-learning procedure to measure each model's adaptability on a small, clean validation set. The final model is a learned combination that can strategically retain or discard information from the oracle based on empirical alignment with the target task, formally bounding the sample complexity even when the oracle is imperfect.

ideas.title: Use adversarial training for realism
ideas.description: Employ GANs to make the decoder's output indistinguishable from real images.

ideas.title: Apply meta-learning for fast model adaptation

ideas.title: Microarchitectural Circuit Theory for Software Performance
ideas.description: This research adapts solution frameworks from condensed matter physics, specifically effective medium and percolation theories, to theorize the impact of software implementation details. In physics, these theories explain how microscopic details (e.g., atomic defects) lead to disproportionate macroscopic material properties. The analogous problem in computing is understanding why specific, localized code optimizations (the microscopic details) can have non-linear, large-scale effects on overall system performance. The adapted idea is to model a software system as a computational medium, formally characterizing how the density, placement, and interaction of specific low-level optimizations (e.g., a particular memory access pattern, a branch prediction hint) percolate to create emergent runtime behavior. The outcome would be a theoretical framework that predicts performance cliffs and speedups based on the statistical distribution of code-level features, moving beyond profiling and towards predictive performance science.

ideas.title: Theoretically formalize smoothness guarantees

ideas.title: Assume benchmarks are always misaligned
ideas.description: Treat all benchmark datasets as flawed proxies and develop methods to infer the true target task.
ideas.sourceTitle: From ImageNet to Image Classification: Contextualizing Progress on Benchmarks

ideas.title: Scalable Empirical Counterfactual Tracing for Edit Targeting
ideas.description: This method directly measures the causal responsibility of training data for faulty model rules by performing efficient, targeted counterfactual re-training. Instead of relying on the unreliable approximations of influence functions, the system uses distributed computation to re-train from checkpoints hundreds of times, each time omitting a different candidate cluster of training examples identified by scalable similarity search. By comparing the resulting models' performance on a targeted validation set that exhibits the faulty rule, it pinpoints the minimal set of training data whose removal most reliably corrects the error, providing a robust and actionable target for model editing.

ideas.title: Curriculum Learning for Robust Opinion Understanding Across Expanding Subjective Dimensions
ideas.description: This research direction proposes training models for subjective opinion understanding using a dual-axis curriculum. The first axis gradually increases the strength and sophistication of adversarial perturbations (e.g., paraphrasing, introducing bias, or adding contradictory viewpoints) applied to opinion statements. The second axis simultaneously expands the training dataset from simpler, more factual opinions to a vast, multi-dimensional OpinionsQA encompassing highly nuanced, culturally contingent, and emotionally charged topics. This structured exposure teaches the model not just to recognize opinions on a fixed set of topics, but to build a fundamental, robust capacity to parse, reason about, and disentangle complex subjective expressions across an open-ended domain.

ideas.title: Theoretical Micro-Optimizations for Perceptual Smoothness on Edge Devices
ideas.description: This research develops a theoretical framework that explains why specific, lightweight code-level changes (e.g., memory access patterns, instruction scheduling, or precision adjustments) have a disproportionate impact on perceptual smoothness metrics like frame-time consistency. The core innovation is using this theory to algorithmically generate a 'minimal sufficient modification set' for a given edge hardware profile, providing a formal guarantee that the implemented optimizations deliver the critical smoothness benefit without superfluous computation. This enables automated, theory-driven tailoring of smoothness optimizations directly to a device's microarchitecture and system constraints, replacing heuristic, one-size-fits-all approaches.

ideas.title: Benchmark performance translates to real-world usefulness
ideas.description: Investigate the assumed direct correlation between outperforming benchmarks and increased usefulness for real-world queries.
ideas.sourceTitle: OpenAI GPT-5 System Card

ideas.title: Invert the goal: minimal data for failure
ideas.description: Find the minimal data needed to guarantee a model is not robust, rather than to learn robustness.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adversarially Robust Generalization Requires More Data

ideas.title: Post-Hoc Rule Attribution and Editing for Certified Debugging
ideas.description: This research direction challenges the necessity of intrinsic interpretability by developing methods that use post-hoc explanations to automatically pinpoint the specific, often localized, decision rules or feature interactions responsible for a critical failure. It then leverages this attribution to surgically edit the model—for example, by updating weights in a subnetwork or modifying a knowledge graph—to correct the behavior. The core synergy is that the automated identification of faulty rules (Idea A) is guided and made actionable by a post-hoc debugging paradigm (Idea B), resulting in a closed-loop system for certified debugging. This allows for the remediation of high-stakes errors in complex, black-box models with a level of precision and assurance previously thought to require interpretable-by-design architectures.

ideas.title: Ablation-Driven Landscape Analysis for RL Generalization
ideas.description: This research inverts the standard RL paper structure by making large-scale, systematic ablation studies the central focus, specifically targeting components hypothesized to affect optimization landscape smoothness (e.g., normalization layers, gradient clipping, entropy regularization). The core innovation is to directly measure the induced smoothness of the loss landscape for each ablated configuration and rigorously correlate these metrics with the generalization gap across diverse environments. This creates a causal experimental framework that moves beyond reporting performance drops to explaining *why* certain components are critical by linking their removal to quantifiable changes in landscape geometry and generalization failure modes.

ideas.title: Dual: generate synthetic data matching target
ideas.description: Instead of selecting existing data, generate new pretraining text that matches the target feature distribution.
ideas.sourceTitle: Data Selection for Language Models via Importance Resampling

ideas.title: Scale up to multi-modal data hierarchies
ideas.description: Create subpopulation shift benchmarks using datasets that combine image, text, and audio under a unified ontology.
ideas.sourceTitle: BREEDS: Benchmarks for Subpopulation Shift

ideas.title: Develop benchmarks accounting for dataset misalignment
ideas.description: Create new model evaluation toolkits that explicitly measure and correct for biases introduced by noisy data collection pipelines.
ideas.sourceTitle: From ImageNet to Image Classification: Contextualizing Progress on Benchmarks

ideas.title: Ablation-Driven Concurrent Rule Editing for Robust Multi-Task RL
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel reinforcement learning paradigm where the primary training objective is to perform systematic, concurrent ablations across a policy's entire set of decision rules. Instead of treating ablation studies as a secondary analysis step, the method inverts the process: it continuously runs large-scale, structured ablations to identify which subsets of rules can be safely edited or rewritten in parallel without degrading performance on existing tasks. This ablation-driven search directly informs a concurrent editing mechanism, allowing for efficient, interference-free learning of new skills or adaptations. The result is a system that inherently maintains robustness and interpretability by construction, as every major update is validated by a comprehensive ablation study before being permanently integrated.

ideas.title: Manifold Geometry Dictates Normalization-Specific Robustness Gaps
ideas.description: This research direction develops a theoretical framework to explain why different normalization layers exhibit distinct robustness and generalization gaps by linking these gaps directly to the underlying geometry of the data manifold. It posits that properties like local curvature, feature entanglement, and group symmetry within the data distribution interact uniquely with the statistical aggregation rules of each normalization type, creating layer-specific vulnerabilities. The analysis will derive sample complexity bounds for each normalization that are explicitly tied to these measurable manifold properties, providing a predictive model for which normalization to use for a given dataset structure. Ultimately, this offers principled guidance for selecting and designing normalization layers to minimize robustness gaps based on data characteristics, rather than through empirical trial-and-error.

ideas.title: Multi-Baseline Evaluation Framework for Sparse Explanations
ideas.description: This research introduces a robust evaluation framework that moves beyond a single, flawed dense baseline. It systematically assesses sparse explanation methods against a carefully constructed suite of contrastive baselines, including random feature importance, a constant explanation, and the explanations generated by a simple, inherently interpretable model. This multi-baseline approach allows researchers to precisely quantify whether a sparse explanation provides meaningful signal beyond noise and how its fidelity compares to a known interpretable model's reasoning. The resulting metrics provide a more nuanced and reliable measure of explanation quality, separating truly informative sparse explanations from those that are merely better than a nonsensical dense approximation.

ideas.title: Close capability gaps in real-world scenarios
ideas.description: Improve model performance in complex, open-ended real-world tasks where it is less capable than humans.
ideas.sourceTitle: GPT-4 Technical Report

ideas.title: Post-Training Model Editing via Constraint-Enforcing Rewriting, Inspired by Structural Engineering
ideas.description: This research seed proposes using direct rewriting to enforce fairness or robustness constraints on a trained model. Drawing an analogy from structural engineering, where buildings are retrofitted with targeted modifications (e.g., adding dampers, bracing) to meet new safety standards without rebuilding, we adapt this solution to machine learning. The adapted idea is to treat the trained model as an existing structure, analyze its computational pathways to locate components responsible for violating a given constraint (e.g., demographic parity or adversarial vulnerability), and then surgically edit or add small, targeted parameter adjustments to enforce the constraint. This post-training 'retrofitting' approach aims to directly embed the new property with minimal disruption to the model's original performance on its primary task, providing a efficient alternative to full retraining or in-training regularization.

ideas.title: Develop algorithms approaching robust sample complexity
ideas.description: The original idea aims to design training algorithms that achieve the information-theoretic lower bound for robust sample complexity. Drawing an analogy from Communication Theory, the analogous problem of achieving the Shannon capacity limit for noisy channels was solved by iterative, probabilistic decoding algorithms (e.g., turbo codes, LDPC codes) that use belief propagation on graphical models. The adapted research idea is to treat the problem of learning from adversarially perturbed data as a decoding problem over a 'data perturbation channel.' We propose to design novel training algorithms that construct a factor graph relating training samples, model parameters, and possible adversarial manipulations. Using message-passing or belief-propagation inspired updates on this graph, the algorithm would iteratively and jointly infer the robust model parameters and the most challenging data corruptions, aiming to efficiently match the fundamental sample complexity limits for robust learning, much like iterative decoders approach channel capacity.

ideas.title: Leverage control theory for gradient regulation
ideas.description: Frame BatchNorm as a feedback controller regulating gradient magnitude and direction.
ideas.sourceTitle: How Does Batch Normalization Help Optimization? (No, It Is Not About Internal Covariate Shift)

ideas.title: Predictive Micro-Architectural Modeling for Code Optimization Impact
ideas.description: This research develops a formal, parameterized model of the underlying hardware execution environment (e.g., caches, pipelines, predictors) and system software stack. The core methodology uses this model to generate quantitative, pre-implementation predictions about the performance effects of specific code-level changes, moving beyond post-hoc explanation. These predictions are then validated through controlled experimentation, and discrepancies are used to iteratively refine the model's accuracy and generalizability across different hardware generations and compiler versions.

ideas.title: Scale down to micro-edits on single neurons
ideas.description: Refine the technique to make ultra-localized edits to individual neurons or tiny subnetworks.
ideas.sourceTitle: Editing a classifier by rewriting its prediction rules

ideas.title: Human-Guided Minimal Edits: Measuring Trust in Sparse Explanations for Constrained Model Debugging
ideas.description: This research direction develops a methodology to find minimal, interpretable edits to a model's behavior (e.g., fixing a specific failure mode) while explicitly measuring and optimizing for human trust. We conduct human-subject studies where participants are presented with sparse explanations for both the original model's failure and candidate 'patches' generated by a constrained editing algorithm. By quantifying how different explanation sparsity and edit types affect user trust and debugging efficiency, we can train or refine the editing algorithm to propose changes that are not only functionally minimal but also maximally trustworthy and comprehensible to human overseers. This creates a human-in-the-loop framework for model debugging that jointly optimizes for algorithmic minimality and psychological usability.

ideas.title: Leveraging Ecological Niche Theory to Test Interpretability via Feature Grouping
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that sparsity is the primary driver of interpretability by drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecology, complex systems are made interpretable by grouping species into functional groups based on shared roles, rather than analyzing them in sparse isolation. The adapted idea is to apply this 'functional grouping' principle to machine learning features. We will develop a method to cluster a model's internal features (e.g., CNN activations) into higher-level, semantically coherent groups. A controlled human-subject study will then test if explanations generated from these grouped features lead to higher ratings of interpretability, trust, and understanding compared to explanations derived from the original, sparse feature set.

ideas.title: Target data is fully representative and available
ideas.description: Relax the need for a large, clean target sample by using weak supervision or synthetic targets.
ideas.sourceTitle: Data Selection for Language Models via Importance Resampling

ideas.title: Assume no shared prior between encoder and decoder
ideas.description: Design a system where the generative model is not pre-trained or shared as a prior.
ideas.sourceTitle: Generative Compression

ideas.title: Apply game theory to feature competition
ideas.description: Model the learning of robust and non-robust features as a game between different network components.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adversarial Examples Are Not Bugs, They Are Features

ideas.title: Apply curriculum learning for robustness
ideas.description: Gradually increase perturbation strength during training, akin to curriculum learning, to ease the learning problem.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adversarially Robust Generalization Requires More Data

ideas.title: A Taxonomic Framework and Compendium for RL Code-Level Optimizations
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from pharmacology's chemical database problem to systematically catalog code-level RL optimizations. In pharmacology, scattered data on bioactive compounds was unified into structured databases with standardized representations and rich metadata. Similarly, this project will create a comprehensive, taxonomic inventory of optimization 'recipes' found in major RL implementations (e.g., from OpenAI Baselines, Stable-Baselines3, RLlib). Each entry will be a standardized profile detailing the optimization's purpose, code snippet, applicable algorithms, hyperparameter configurations, empirical performance effects documented in literature, and potential synergies or conflicts with other optimizations. The resulting compendium will serve as a foundational resource for reproducible RL research, automated algorithm configuration, and education, transforming ad-hoc engineering knowledge into a searchable, analyzable corpus.

ideas.title: Explicit user intent reliably triggers deeper reasoning
ideas.description: Test if phrases like 'think hard' are reliable signals for routing to a deeper reasoning model.
ideas.sourceTitle: OpenAI GPT-5 System Card

ideas.title: Inverse problem: find target given model data
ideas.description: Given a pretrained model and its data, infer the implicit target distribution it was optimized for.
ideas.sourceTitle: Data Selection for Language Models via Importance Resampling

ideas.title: Downscale for data selection in low-resource settings
ideas.description: Adapt DSIR for scenarios with extremely limited raw data or computational budget for feature estimation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Data Selection for Language Models via Importance Resampling

ideas.title: Lightweight smoothness for edge devices
ideas.description: Create a minimal-computation method that provides only the critical smoothness benefit.
ideas.sourceTitle: How Does Batch Normalization Help Optimization? (No, It Is Not About Internal Covariate Shift)

ideas.title: Sparse Coding for Identifying Data Models with Minimal Robustness Gap
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using sparse coding as a diagnostic tool to find and analyze data distributions where the sample complexity for standard and robust learning is similar. By applying sparse coding to disentangle features, we can directly measure the degree of entanglement between robust and non-robust features in a given dataset. This allows us to theoretically and empirically characterize the properties of data (e.g., sparsity, orthogonality) that naturally force these feature sets apart, minimizing the robustness gap. The core novelty is using a representation learning technique not just for model improvement, but as an analytical framework to explain the fundamental difficulty of robust learning across different data domains.

ideas.title: Blind Source Separation for Model Editing
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a known vector direction for a spurious feature is necessary for model editing. By drawing an analogy to blind source separation in signal processing—where techniques like Independent Component Analysis (ICA) recover original signals from mixtures without prior knowledge of the sources—we adapt this approach to machine learning models. The adapted research idea proposes to treat a neural network's internal representations as a mixture of independent latent factors (e.g., core features and spurious features). By analyzing the statistics of activations on a dataset designed to vary the spurious correlation, we can algorithmically isolate the direction corresponding to the unwanted feature without it being pre-defined. This 'blind' separation then allows for targeted model editing, such as projection or intervention, to mitigate the spurious association, relaxing a key prerequisite in current editing methods.

ideas.title: Adapting Topological Data Analysis for Non-Linear Decision Boundary Editing
ideas.description: This idea generalizes the direct rewriting method for classifiers to handle non-linear decision boundaries by drawing an analogy from Topological Data Analysis (TDA). In TDA, the complex shape of high-dimensional data is summarized using tools like persistent homology, which tracks topological features across scales via a filtration of a simplicial complex. The adapted solution applies this framework to the classifier's prediction landscape: construct a simplicial complex over the input space based on the model's predictions, use prediction confidence or boundary distance as a filtration parameter, and compute a persistence diagram that captures the essential 'topology' of the non-linear decision rule (e.g., isolated high-confidence clusters or intricate boundary loops). This topological summary then serves as a guide for editing operations, allowing a user to specify desired changes to these global, geometric features (e.g., 'merge these two prediction islands' or 'smooth out this loop in the boundary'), which would be translated into targeted parameter updates or data augmentations for the model, thereby enabling the editing of complex, non-linear prediction rules through a geometric, topology-aware lens.

ideas.title: Apply control theory for stable model scaling
ideas.description: Use control theory principles to manage and stabilize training dynamics in extremely large models.
ideas.sourceTitle: GPT-4 Technical Report

ideas.title: Pre-training for Symbolic Rule Induction via Auxiliary Objectives
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel pre-training framework that integrates symbolic regression as an auxiliary objective alongside or in place of standard next-token prediction. The model is trained not only to predict sequences but to simultaneously search for and internally represent the underlying data-generating processes as compact mathematical expressions or symbolic rules. This forces the model to develop latent representations that explicitly encode causal or mechanistic relationships, moving beyond statistical correlation. The resulting pre-trained model would possess enhanced interpretability, robustness to distribution shifts, and a stronger inductive bias for discovering fundamental laws from observational data.

ideas.title: Use program synthesis for rule-based explanations
ideas.description: Generate human-readable rules from sparse weights, similar to symbolic regression.
ideas.sourceTitle: Leveraging Sparse Linear Layers for Debuggable Deep Networks

ideas.title: Inverse problem: generate inputs from explanations
ideas.description: Synthesize prototypical or counterfactual inputs that activate specific sparse explanation features.
ideas.sourceTitle: Leveraging Sparse Linear Layers for Debuggable Deep Networks

ideas.title: Certified Robustness Regions for Classifier Editing via Dynamic Invariance Verification
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for certifying the performance and stability of classifiers after direct rule rewriting against distributional shift and adversarial inputs. It combines symbolic verification of the edit's logical implications with empirical measurement of the model's behavior on a curated suite of stress-test data designed to probe compositional failures. The core output is a dynamically defined 'region of certified robustness' for the edit, providing formal, data-informed bounds on where the edit will hold and where performance may degrade. This moves theoretical guarantees from a static, closed-world assumption to a practical, reliability-focused certificate for real-world deployment.

ideas.title: Adapt curriculum learning for safety training
ideas.description: Employ curriculum learning strategies from machine learning to structure the safe-completions safety training process.
ideas.sourceTitle: OpenAI GPT-5 System Card

ideas.title: Minimax Sample Complexity under Resource-Constrained Sequential Adversaries
ideas.description: This research formalizes and analyzes robust learning when data is generated by a sequential adversary with explicitly bounded resources. It models the interaction as a multi-round game where the adversary, constrained by limits on memory, computational power, or temporal dependence, can adaptively corrupt or generate samples to hinder learning. The core objective is to derive minimax optimal sample complexity bounds for fundamental problems (e.g., PAC learning, regression) as a function of the adversary's constraint parameters. This yields a precise hierarchy of robustness, showing how much additional data is required to compensate for specific, quantifiable forms of non-i.i.d. manipulation.

ideas.title: Quantify human trust in sparse explanations
ideas.description: Conduct larger-scale human studies to measure how explanations affect user trust and debugging efficiency.
ideas.sourceTitle: Leveraging Sparse Linear Layers for Debuggable Deep Networks

ideas.title: Automated Fault Localization for Model Rules via Spectrum-Based Debugging
ideas.description: This research adapts the principle of Spectrum-Based Fault Localization (SFL) from software debugging to the problem of identifying problematic prediction rules in AI models. In SFL, the execution of code elements is tracked across many test runs, and statistical techniques (e.g., Ochiai coefficient) correlate element activity with test failures to rank suspicious code. Analogously, for a model whose behavior can be traced to the application of specific rules (e.g., in a symbolic reasoning layer, a decision list, or an interpretable classifier), we propose instrumenting the model to log which rules are activated during inference on a curated dataset containing both desired and undesired model outputs. By analyzing this 'rule activation spectrum' and correlating it with the occurrence of errors or undesired behaviors, the method can automatically compute a ranked list of rules most likely responsible for the faults. This provides a systematic, automated starting point for model editors, significantly narrowing the search space from the entire rule set to a high-probability subset requiring intervention.

ideas.title: Econometric Valuation of Explanations for Optimal Data Investment
ideas.description: This research integrates sparse model explanations directly into the training loop to penalize spurious features, but then applies econometric techniques—such as instrumental variables or production function estimation—to this process. The goal is to measure the marginal return on investment (ROI) of implementing such explanation-based debiasing, compared to the ROI of simply scaling dataset size or quality. This allows for the creation of a unified economic model that dictates the most cost-effective strategy for achieving model robustness: whether to invest computational resources in sophisticated training-time interventions or in acquiring additional data, based on their quantified diminishing returns.

ideas.title: A unified two-model system is optimal for scaling
ideas.description: Question if a unified system with main and thinking models is the best architecture for cost and quality.
ideas.sourceTitle: OpenAI GPT-5 System Card

ideas.title: Geometrically-Regularized Constrained Optimization for Coherent Model Editing
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond direct rewriting to a principled optimization framework for editing complex, non-linear predictors. It formulates the edit as a constraint within an objective that minimizes the global functional change, preserving the model's behavior on unrelated inputs. A novel geometric regularizer, derived from the Riemannian curvature of the model's decision manifold, is introduced to prevent pathological boundary distortions. The result is a robust editing mechanism that guarantees the intended local edit while maintaining the global coherence and performance of the original classifier.

ideas.title: Causal Discovery for Non-Robust Feature Attribution in NLP
ideas.description: This research direction applies causal discovery and inference methods from Idea A to the search for non-robust features in NLP from Idea B. The core objective is to develop a framework that can distinguish whether a spurious linguistic correlation (e.g., a specific phrase or punctuation pattern) is a genuine cause of a model's prediction or merely a coincidental artifact of the training data. This enables the creation of more robust and interpretable feature attributions for complex NLP models by providing causal, not just correlational, explanations for their susceptibility to adversarial examples or distribution shifts. The outcome is a principled tool for diagnosing and mitigating reliance on harmful spurious features in text-based AI systems.

ideas.title: Apply DSIR to dynamic or streaming data sources
ideas.description: Adapt importance resampling for continuously updating raw datasets and shifting target distributions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Data Selection for Language Models via Importance Resampling

ideas.title: Use influence functions to explain feature importance
ideas.description: Combine sparse explanations with training data influence for richer debugging.
ideas.sourceTitle: Leveraging Sparse Linear Layers for Debuggable Deep Networks

ideas.title: Applying Topological Data Analysis to Edit Entangled Neural Features
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that model editing requires a target feature to be linearly separable in the representation space. Drawing an analogy from computational topology, where persistent homology solves the problem of characterizing complex, entangled structures in high-dimensional data, we adapt this solution to neural network editing. The adapted idea applies topological data analysis (TDA) to the model's activation space to identify the persistent topological features (like clusters or manifolds) that define an entangled concept. Model editing is then reconceptualized as performing a targeted topological deformation—such as shifting, merging, or separating these persistent structures—rather than applying a linear transformation, enabling the direct editing of non-linearly separable features.

ideas.title: Dual: sample complexity for robust feature extraction
ideas.description: Study the data needed to learn robust features alone, decoupled from the final classification layer.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adversarially Robust Generalization Requires More Data

ideas.title: Scale down to few-class or toy datasets
ideas.description: Apply the BREEDS methodology to small, synthetic datasets to enable rapid prototyping of robustness algorithms.

ideas.title: Apply concept bottleneck models for debugging

ideas.sourceTitle: Leveraging Sparse Linear Layers for Debuggable Deep Networks

ideas.title: Minimal Data for Guaranteed Robustness Gain
ideas.description: This research constructs a method to find the minimal, most informative data points required to guarantee a measurable improvement in model robustness, shifting from a destructive to a constructive goal. It begins by characterizing the model's vulnerability space, similar to the original idea, but then uses this to query or synthesize a minimal set of corrective examples. The core algorithm iteratively selects data that maximally closes the robustness gap identified by the failure conditions. The output is both a theoretical bound on data efficiency for robustness training and a practical, sample-efficient data acquisition strategy for building reliable models.

ideas.title: Theorize impact of implementation details
ideas.description: Develop theoretical models explaining why specific code-level optimizations have such large effects.
ideas.sourceTitle: Implementation Matters in Deep Policy Gradients: A Case Study on PPO and TRPO

ideas.title: Intent-Triggered Synthesis for Self-Correcting, Interpretable Reasoning
ideas.description: This research direction investigates a novel feedback loop between user intent and model self-improvement. When a user employs a triggering phrase signaling a need for deeper reasoning, the system is routed to a specialized module that uses program synthesis techniques to generate an explicit, interpretable rule or logical program representing its reasoning path. This synthesized rule is then presented to the user alongside the final answer, allowing for verification and critique. Crucially, the system can use this interaction—comparing its synthesized rule against user feedback or ground truth—to automatically rewrite and correct its internal rule representations, leading to more reliable and transparent reasoning over time.

ideas.title: Smoothness is primary benefit
ideas.description: Investigate if other undiscussed BatchNorm properties are equally or more important.
ideas.sourceTitle: How Does Batch Normalization Help Optimization? (No, It Is Not About Internal Covariate Shift)

ideas.title: Forensic Attribution Benchmarking for Optimization Artifacts
ideas.description: This research creates a novel benchmark suite designed for the forensic analysis of optimization contributions in image processing and computer vision pipelines. It adapts techniques from digital forensics, such as error level analysis, sensor noise fingerprinting, and compression artifact detection, to systematically characterize and catalog the unique artifact signatures left by different optimization methods (e.g., model pruning, quantization, compiler flags). The benchmark provides a dual output: a quantitative performance score across diverse environments and a forensic report detailing the artifact 'fingerprint' of the optimization, enabling developers to trace performance gains directly to the visual or structural compromises introduced.

ideas.title: Dual problem: Optimize for cost over speed
ideas.description: Design a router that prioritizes minimizing computational cost rather than maximizing response speed for queries.
ideas.sourceTitle: OpenAI GPT-5 System Card

ideas.title: Quantitative Attribution Framework for Algorithm vs. Systems Performance Gains
ideas.description: This research develops a methodology to systematically decompose and attribute performance improvements in computational systems. It establishes a controlled benchmarking suite that runs identical algorithmic cores across varied systems-level implementations (from naive to highly optimized). By measuring performance deltas under strict isolation of variables, the framework produces quantitative evidence for when and how much gains stem from pure algorithmic complexity versus hardware-aware systems engineering. The outcome is a set of attribution principles that can guide research investment and architectural design.

ideas.title: Router decisions must be based on conversation signals
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that a router must use conversation type, complexity, and tool needs for decisions.
ideas.sourceTitle: OpenAI GPT-5 System Card

ideas.title: Dynamic Concept Discovery and Bottleneck Models for Open-World Debugging
ideas.description: This system augments human-defined concept bottlenecks with a dynamic discovery module that automatically identifies candidate concepts from model failure patterns and internal representations. These data-driven candidates are presented to a human operator for validation, refinement, and optional addition to the active concept set. The resulting hybrid bottleneck enables debugging for both predefined semantic concepts and novel, emergent failure modes not initially specified. This creates a continuously improving, closed-loop framework for model interpretation and correction.

ideas.title: Non-robust features are always undesirable

ideas.title: Robust Sequential Decision-Making via Ecological Stability Analysis
ideas.description: This research seed proposes extending sample complexity analysis to robust sequential decision tasks (RL or prediction) by drawing an analogy from theoretical ecology. In ecology, the stability of ecosystems under parameter perturbations is analyzed using tools like Lyapunov exponents to separate nominal dynamics from robustness to shocks. We adapt this solution by framing a Markov Decision Process (MDP) as a dynamical system. The sample complexity for robust RL can then be analyzed by decomposing it into the base complexity of learning the nominal system plus a 'stability premium.' This premium scales with the size of the prescribed uncertainty set and the system's inherent sensitivity to perturbations (quantified via adapted Lyapunov exponents), addressing the SCALING_DUAL dimension of scaling with both data and robustness requirements.

ideas.title: Causal Feature Isolation and Constrained Non-Linear Editing
ideas.description: This approach first develops a robust, non-linear probing framework to causally identify the specific entangled feature subspace within a model's representations, moving beyond linear separability. Using this probe as a precise objective, the system then performs a targeted model edit via a constrained optimization that applies minimal, rank-constrained parameter updates. This ensures the edit successfully manipulates the complex target feature while actively preserving performance on other tasks by regularizing changes to remain within the isolated feature direction. The result is a principled methodology for editing highly entangled concepts with measurable specificity and safety.

ideas.title: Theoretically analyze DSIR's convergence and guarantees
ideas.description: Provide formal convergence rates and error bounds for the importance resampling procedure in high dimensions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Data Selection for Language Models via Importance Resampling

ideas.sourceTitle: Adversarial Examples Are Not Bugs, They Are Features

ideas.title: Sparse Genomic Signal Detection in Pixel Space via Compressed Sensing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from genomics for detecting sparse signals in massive search spaces to the problem of applying sparse linear explainers directly to raw pixel space. In genomics, the analogous problem of finding a few causal variants among millions of SNPs is solved by combining dimensionality reduction/compression with sparse regression. Adapted to computer vision, the research idea involves a pipeline where the massive pixel feature vector is first compressed via a fast, information-preserving transform (e.g., a learned or random projection into a lower-dimensional space where salient structures are retained). A sparse linear explainer (like an L1-penalized regression) is then applied efficiently within this compressed space to identify a small set of critical pixel 'components.' The resulting sparse weights are then mapped back to the original pixel space, providing an interpretable saliency map, thereby making explainability in massive feature spaces computationally tractable.

ideas.title: Dual: make non-robust features robust
ideas.description: Explore if non-robust features can be made perceptible and understandable to humans through transformation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adversarial Examples Are Not Bugs, They Are Features

ideas.title: Create a single model with dynamic compute
ideas.description: Develop a single model that internally allocates variable compute per query, eliminating the need for a router.
ideas.sourceTitle: OpenAI GPT-5 System Card

ideas.title: Causal Steering for Localized, Robust Editing of Foundation Model Behaviors
ideas.description: This research develops a method to precisely edit specific behaviors in large foundation models without causing widespread unintended side-effects. It learns a sparse causal model of how high-level concepts are represented in the model's internal activations, enabling targeted interventions rather than blunt parameter rewrites. The system uses a guardrail module that continuously evaluates the impact of an edit on a broad distribution of held-out tasks, automatically rejecting edits that degrade performance. This results in a scalable, robust editing framework that can reliably update model 'rules' while preserving overall coherence and capability.
