ideas.title: Causal Ensemble Intervention Networks
ideas.description: This research direction develops methods to apply causal interventions to the internal mechanisms (e.g., specific feature representations or attention heads) of all models within an ensemble in a coordinated fashion. Instead of editing each model's predictions independently, the approach uses causal graphs derived from the ensemble's combined structure to identify key intervention points that propagate edits coherently. This allows for the enforcement of global, domain-aware invariances or the removal of spurious correlations from the entire ensemble's decision-making process at once, leading to more robust and causally-aligned system behavior than editing models individually or in isolation.

ideas.title: Adversarial Multi-Fidelity Invariance Testing for Robust Spurious Correlation Detection
ideas.description: This system addresses the circularity of relying on a single explanation method by implementing an adversarial, multi-fidelity testing framework. It generates a diverse suite of synthetic perturbations and counterfactual data slices to probe model predictions from multiple explanatory angles. By applying formal causal invariance tests across these varied contexts, it identifies features whose attributed importance is unstable or dependent on spurious data artifacts. This approach provides a robust, automated audit that flags potential spurious correlations based on explanatory inconsistency rather than trust in any single interpretability tool.

ideas.title: Apply Adaptive Clinical Trial Design to Active Learning for Robust Data Collection
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from adaptive clinical trial design to the problem of using active learning to collect robust data. In clinical trials, adaptive designs solve the analogous problem of efficiently allocating limited patient resources to learn the most about treatment efficacy and safety, using interim analyses to dynamically guide the trial. The adapted research idea applies this by designing an active learning framework that performs periodic 'interim analyses' on a growing dataset. Instead of querying based solely on standard uncertainty, the algorithm would evaluate and re-estimate which types of samples (e.g., those near perceived decision boundaries under perturbation, or from underrepresented subpopulations critical for worst-case performance) are most informative for reducing robust generalization error. The sampling strategy is then dynamically adjusted—much like reallocating patients in a trial—to prioritize querying from these high-impact regions, creating a more efficient and targeted process for building robust models with limited labeling budget.

ideas.title: A Diagnostic Framework for Sufficient Explanations: From Sparsity to Structure
ideas.description: This research develops a task-driven, diagnostic framework to determine when sparse linear models are sufficient for explanation and when they fail. It begins by formalizing quantitative objectives for an explanation, such as enabling correct counterfactual reasoning or identifying robust causal features. The core system then employs a two-stage process: first, it rigorously evaluates the fidelity and stability of a sparse linear explainer against a high-accuracy predictive model; second, if the linear explainer is deficient, it uses targeted perturbation and interaction detection methods to diagnose the precise source of insufficiency (e.g., specific feature interactions or non-linearities). The output is not just a binary answer but a structured report that prescribes the minimal model complexity required for a faithful explanation in that context.

ideas.title: Automated Spurious Correlation Detection for Robust Fallback Model Validation
ideas.description: This research develops a framework that uses automated spurious correlation detection algorithms, originally designed for model interpretability, to critically evaluate the 'mini' or fallback models deployed after primary model usage limits are reached. It challenges the assumption of adequacy by programmatically testing whether the fallback model's sparse explanations reveal a reliance on different, and potentially more spurious, features than the primary model. This enables an automated, evidence-based assessment of fallback model robustness and trustworthiness, moving beyond simple performance metrics. The outcome is a system that can flag when a fallback model is operationally unsafe due to shifted inference patterns, guiding the development of more reliable and conscientiously designed scaled-down systems.

ideas.title: Edit black-box models without internal access

ideas.title: Conversation-Aware Knowledge Graph Routing: Using Ontological Constraints to Derive Routing Signals
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel routing paradigm where a conversational AI's router does not rely on pre-defined, static categories like 'conversation type' or 'complexity'. Instead, it uses a live, structured knowledge graph of the conversation's domain to infer necessary routing signals. The ontological constraints and logical relationships within the graph—such as prerequisite knowledge, entity dependencies, and procedural steps—actively guide and constrain the generation of routing rules. This results in a router whose decisions are fundamentally explainable by the domain's ontology and are dynamically aligned with the conversation's current semantic context and logical trajectory.

ideas.title: Adapting Clinical Trial Protocols for Ablation Study Design in Deep RL
ideas.description: This idea proposes to formalize ablation study methodology in deep RL by drawing an analogy from clinical trial design in pharmacology. The analogous problem—isolating the causal effect of a single drug component from a complex mixture—is solved using standardized, blinded, factorial protocols. Adapting this solution involves creating a rigorous experimental framework for RL: defining a baseline 'control' algorithm, establishing procedures to vary only one component (e.g., a specific network module or reward shaping term) while holding all else constant (including hyperparameters, random seeds, and environmental stochasticity), and introducing evaluation 'blinding' where possible to prevent bias. The resulting methodology would provide a checklist and reporting standard, akin to a CONSORT statement for clinical trials, ensuring that ablation studies in RL are reproducible, comparable, and truly informative about component contributions.

ideas.title: Dual: sample complexity for robust feature extraction via statistical mechanics of disordered systems
ideas.description: This research studies the data needed to learn robust features alone, decoupled from the final classification layer. It draws an analogy from statistical physics, specifically the analysis of disordered systems like spin glasses, where methods like the replica and cavity approaches are used to characterize the system's stable equilibrium states independently of any specific external field. By adapting this framework, the research formulates robust feature learning as finding stable, low-complexity states in a disordered data system. The goal is to derive sample complexity bounds for feature extraction based on the entropy and stability of the learned representation manifold, providing a theoretical scaling law that depends solely on the properties of the feature extractor and data distribution, not the downstream task.

ideas.title: Experimentally Identified Causal Mechanisms for Robust Feature Editing
ideas.description: This approach addresses the causal identification problem by actively learning an approximate causal graph of a model's internal mechanisms through systematic perturbation. It employs a discovery phase that uses controlled interventions on neurons, layers, or attention heads to collect data for causal structure learning, moving beyond purely observational correlation. The resulting probabilistic causal model is then used to plan targeted edits that maximize the intended effect while minimizing side-effects, with each edit validated through counterfactual simulation. This creates a closed-loop system for principled, auditable, and robust model editing grounded in empirically tested causal relationships.

ideas.title: Post-Hoc Forensic Analysis as Sufficient for System Debugging
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that intrinsic interpretability is necessary for debugging critical AI systems by drawing an analogy from forensic engineering. In fields like structural or aircraft failure analysis, engineers successfully diagnose root causes through post-hoc forensic methods—meticulously examining physical evidence, data recorders, and simulation—without requiring a complete, real-time intrinsic model of the system's internal forces prior to the event. Adapted to AI, the research idea proposes that true debuggability can be achieved by enforcing rigorous, high-fidelity logging (an 'AI black box recorder') of all model inputs, internal activation traces, and outputs. Debugging then becomes a forensic science of reconstructing the causal chain leading to a failure using these immutable logs and controlled simulation experiments, potentially rendering intrinsic, real-time interpretability unnecessary for critical system validation and repair.

ideas.title: Adapting Fault Tree Analysis from Safety Engineering for Influence-Based Edit Targeting
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodology of Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) from Systems Safety Engineering to the problem of using influence functions for edit targeting in machine learning models. In safety engineering, FTA is used to systematically trace a high-level system failure back to combinations of root causes. Analogously, we propose to treat a specific, undesirable model behavior (e.g., a biased prediction or a logical inconsistency) as the 'top event' failure. The adapted solution involves using influence function analysis not merely to rank influential training examples, but to algorithmically construct a causal-probabilistic graph—a 'training data fault tree.' This tree would model how groups of training examples, weighted by their computed influence and interaction, probabilistically lead to the learned faulty rule. This structured approach would provide a principled way to prioritize and combine targeted edits (e.g., removing or correcting specific training points) by identifying the most critical pathways of influence within the training dataset that are responsible for the model's error.

ideas.title: Clinically-Grounded Debugging: A Validation-First Framework for Medical AI
ideas.description: This research proposes a new framework that embeds clinical validation as a core, non-bypassable component of the debuggability process for medical AI. The system first establishes a set of ground-truth clinical constraints and plausibility checks derived from medical literature and expert consultation. During model development and debugging, every explanation or intermediate representation generated by the model is subjected to these constraints; failures trigger specific debugging pathways rather than generic ones. The final output is a model whose debuggability directly serves the goal of certifying its clinical validity and safety, moving beyond mere interpretability to demonstrably trustworthy reasoning.

ideas.title: Contextual Interpretability: Disentangling the Effects of Feature Sparsity, Structure, and User Expertise on Human Understanding
ideas.description: This research tests the hypothesis that the relationship between feature sparsity and human interpretability is moderated by user expertise and task domain. The study decomposes interpretability into quantifiable sub-measures like simulatability, counterfactual reasoning accuracy, and confidence. In a controlled experiment, participants of varying expertise perform tasks using models with sparse, grouped, or hierarchical feature representations across different domains. The design uses factorial analysis and causal graphs to determine not just if, but for whom, when, and on which interpretability dimension sparsity is beneficial or detrimental.

ideas.title: Zero-Shot Personalized Opinion Editing
ideas.description: This research direction develops methods to edit a base language model to mimic the nuanced opinion profile of a specific individual, without using any of that individual's textual responses as validation or guidance data. Instead, it leverages techniques from rule-based model editing—applied to abstract opinion representations or belief vectors inferred from the LM's own knowledge—to directly sculpt a personalized opinion agent. The core challenge and novelty lie in defining and applying these edits in a zero-shot manner for a new individual, using only a declarative summary of their stances (e.g., from a survey or interview transcript) as the edit specification, thereby bypassing the need for a paired dataset of the individual's text for fine-tuning or validation.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Symbolic Regression for Robust Rule Discovery
ideas.description: This approach embeds symbolic regression within a framework that enforces domain knowledge as hard and soft constraints during the search process. Candidate expressions are evaluated not just on data fit but also on adherence to known physical principles, dimensional consistency, and stability under perturbation. The system employs a multi-objective optimization to balance accuracy, complexity, and physical plausibility, followed by causal validation tests. This yields interpretable mathematical rules that are robust, generalizable, and trustworthy for correction or implementation.

ideas.title: Causal Edit Abduction: Parsimonious Rule Inference from Behavioral Deltas
ideas.description: This system takes a model's behavioral delta—the structured difference in its outputs before and after an edit—and infers the most probable underlying rule change. It operates by generating a constrained space of possible edits using a causal model of the network's relevant computation and a prior favoring simple, localized modifications. Candidates are iteratively refined and filtered by requiring they reproduce the core behavioral shift while minimizing collateral changes on a broad validation set. The output is a ranked set of parsimonious, human-interpretable edit hypotheses that are causally consistent with the observed change.

ideas.title: Diversity-Constrained Joint Editing for Robust Ensembles
ideas.description: This method applies coordinated updates to an ensemble of classifiers while explicitly preserving and encouraging functional diversity among the members. The editing objective combines task-specific correction (e.g., removing a shared bias) with a penalty that minimizes correlation increases in the models' predictions on a separate, unlabeled dataset. This ensures the ensemble's error-reducing capacity is maintained post-edit. The approach provides a principled way to correct systematic flaws in multiple models without collapsing them into a single, less robust effective predictor.

ideas.title: Use epidemiological contact tracing to structure influence-based feature explanation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the epidemiological solution of contact tracing to the problem of explaining feature importance. In the original ML idea, sparse feature attributions (like SHAP or LIME) are combined with training data influence for richer debugging, but the connection between influential examples and specific feature importance in a prediction can be opaque. The analogous field is epidemiology, where contact tracing maps infection spread from source to secondary cases by combining individual patient data with interaction networks. The adapted solution is to treat a model's prediction error as an outbreak. First, use influence functions to identify the most influential training examples ('index cases'). Then, implement a form of 'feature-level contact tracing' that propagates this influence through the model's computational graph to pinpoint the specific features in the new input that served as the critical pathways ('transmission vectors') connecting the influential training data to the final erroneous prediction. This creates a unified, causal-style explanation that links problematic training data directly to the feature-level manifestations in a faulty prediction, offering a richer debugging narrative.

ideas.title: Validation-Free Model Editing via Self-Consistency Landscapes
ideas.description: This approach performs rule rewriting without any external validation dataset by constructing an internal self-consistency check. It generates a synthetic distribution of probe inputs to map the model's behavior before a proposed edit, then enforces that the edit must maximize a combination of edit success on target cases and behavioral preservation on this automatically derived consistency landscape. The system is optimized to find edits that satisfy these intrinsic locality and logical coherence constraints, providing a robust, data-free alternative to validation-guided editing.

ideas.title: Cultural Consensus Editing: Micro-Editing Language Models to Align with Inferred Shared Beliefs
ideas.description: This research direction combines ultra-localized model editing with cultural consensus theory (CCT) from anthropology. First, CCT is applied to analyze response patterns from both a target human population and the language model to infer a formal model of their shared and divergent beliefs. Then, the technique of micro-editing individual neurons or tiny subnetworks is used to surgically alter the model's internal representations, specifically to increase its alignment with the inferred cultural consensus while minimizing disruption to other knowledge. This creates a methodology for culturally calibrating models in a targeted, interpretable, and efficient manner, moving beyond broad fine-tuning to precise conceptual alignment.

ideas.title: Applying Genome Editing Techniques to Structured Prediction Models
ideas.description: This research adapts the classifier editing framework to sequential or structured prediction tasks (e.g., sequence labeling, parsing) by drawing an analogy from synthetic biology and genome engineering. The analogous solution is genome editing techniques like CRISPR-Cas9, where a guide RNA targets a specific DNA sequence for a precise, localized modification by an editor protein. We adapt this by proposing a method where a 'constraint guide' identifies a faulty sub-structure in a model's structured prediction, and a localized 'model editor' performs a surgical correction on that sub-structure. This approach allows for targeted, post-hoc error correction in complex model outputs—such as fixing a consistent mislabeling of a named entity type in a sequence or correcting a specific syntactic attachment error in a parse tree—without retraining the entire model and while ensuring the edited output remains globally coherent.

ideas.title: Causal Ideological Editing: Using Interventions to Align Models with Latent Value Constructs
ideas.description: This research direction combines causal inference for feature editing with a psychographic/ideological evaluation framework. It develops methods to use causal discovery and intervention techniques (e.g., do-calculus, path-specific effects) not on demographic correlates, but on the latent internal representations of ideological constructs like authoritarianism, individualism, or moral foundations within a model. This allows for precise, mechanistic edits that shift a model's behavioral tendencies along these value dimensions in a controlled, interpretable way. The result is a more nuanced and fundamental approach to AI alignment that operates on the causal structure of values rather than on superficial demographic associations.

ideas.title: Apply grammatical error correction synthesis for rule rewriting
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from Grammatical Error Correction (GEC) in Computational Linguistics. In GEC, systems learn to transform flawed sentences into grammatically correct ones using interpretable edit operations. Adapting this solution, we propose treating imperfect prediction rules (e.g., from a preliminary model) as structured 'sentences' needing correction. We will use program synthesis techniques to build a model that learns from historical examples of rule corrections. This model will automatically generate corrected, interpretable prediction rules by applying a constrained set of semantic edits—such as refining logical operators, modifying feature thresholds, or inserting missing conditions—thereby systematically improving rule accuracy and fidelity while maintaining human-understandable logic.

ideas.title: Version Control for Concurrent Rule Rewriting
ideas.description: This idea addresses the challenge of allowing many agents or processes to rewrite a large number of prediction rules concurrently without catastrophic interference. The analogy is drawn from distributed database systems, which solve the parallel problem of concurrent writes to shared data using mechanisms like conflict-free replicated data types (CRDTs) and version vectors. These systems allow independent, asynchronous updates and resolve conflicts deterministically using logical timestamps or merge functions to ensure eventual consistency. Adapted to this research context, the solution involves treating each rule as a data item that can be edited concurrently. Each edit is tagged with a version vector. A semantic merge function, defined per rule type, automatically reconciles conflicting edits (e.g., by averaging parameter updates or intelligently combining conditions). This creates a system akin to 'version control for rule sets,' enabling massive, concurrent rewriting while guaranteeing the rule base converges to a coherent and useful state without manual conflict resolution or catastrophic performance degradation.

ideas.title: Knowledge Graph-Guided Rule Rewriting via Constraint Satisfaction
ideas.description: This idea adapts the constraint satisfaction approach from computational structural biology, specifically protein folding, to the task of rewriting classifier rules. In protein folding, a vast space of possible 3D structures is efficiently searched by applying hard and soft constraints derived from physical laws and known structural patterns. Analogously, we propose to frame the rule rewriting process as a constraint satisfaction problem where the constraints are derived from an associated knowledge graph's ontology (e.g., type hierarchies, property restrictions, disjointness axioms). Candidate rule modifications are generated, and their logical consistency with these ontological constraints is rigorously evaluated. The search for improved rules is thus guided and pruned by this constraint system, ensuring the final classifier rules are not only performant but also semantically coherent and aligned with the structured domain knowledge.

ideas.title: Fairness-Aware Opinion Steering: Using Constraint Editing to Study and Mitigate LM Influence on Human Beliefs
ideas.description: This research program integrates the technical mechanism of direct model editing with the social science question of LM influence. It proposes a series of controlled experiments where participants from different demographic groups are exposed to LM outputs that have been systematically edited to enforce or violate specific fairness criteria (e.g., counter-stereotypical associations, balanced sentiment). By measuring shifts in participant opinions before and after exposure, the study directly tests the causal impact of model-level fairness properties on human belief formation. This allows for the development of novel 'influence-aware' editing rules designed not only to make a model fairer in a static sense, but to steer its real-world social effects toward desired normative outcomes, such as reducing polarization or correcting group-specific misperceptions.

ideas.title: Automated Spurious Correlation Detection via Causal Graph Pruning
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from epidemiology to automate spurious correlation detection in machine learning. In observational studies, causal DAGs and d-separation are used to prune spurious associations explained by confounding variables. We adapt this by using sparse local explanations (like SHAP/LIME) to construct a preliminary feature importance graph. An algorithm is then developed to automatically flag features whose high importance can be d-separated—i.e., their explanatory power vanishes when conditioning on a detected set of correlated background features or potential confounders in the training data. This provides a principled, automated method to warn model developers about features likely representing spurious statistical relationships rather than robust predictive signals.

ideas.title: Adapting Mendelian Randomization for Causal Feature Attribution in ML
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a key problem in epidemiology—distinguishing correlation from causation—to the context of robust feature attribution in machine learning. In epidemiology, Mendelian Randomization uses genetic variants as instrumental variables to infer causal relationships, overcoming confounding in observational data. The adapted research idea proposes to use a set of 'instrumental features' within a dataset (features whose relationship to the model input is known and exogenous) as instruments. By applying causal inference techniques from instrumental variable analysis, we can estimate the causal effect of an input feature on a model's prediction, thereby refining standard attribution maps. This method would allow practitioners to identify and downweight spurious, correlative features in explanations, leading to attributions that more faithfully represent the model's true causal reasoning.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Aware Causal Attribution via Multi-Fidelity Graph Ensembles
ideas.description: This approach addresses the fundamental fragility of causal discovery by treating the inferred causal graph not as a ground truth, but as a high-uncertainty hypothesis. It employs an ensemble of causal structure learning techniques under varied plausible assumptions to generate a set of candidate causal models. Feature importance is then computed as a distribution across this model set, explicitly quantifying the uncertainty in attributions stemming from causal graph uncertainty. The final output is a robust, consensus-based feature ranking accompanied by stability scores, clearly distinguishing features with reliable causal support from those whose importance is highly sensitive to unresolved causal ambiguities.
